issue_title: yq throws TyperError datetime.datetime on ISOFormat strings in yaml file
issue_body:
the default yaml loader will parse ISOFormat strings and create datetime objects which doesn't jsonify well.

---

Example:
```
$ yq --version
usage: yq [-h] [--yaml-output] [--width WIDTH] jq_filter [file [file ...]]
yq: error: too few arguments
$ pip freeze | grep yq
yq==2.1.2
$ yq . sec.yaml
yq: Error while running jq: TypeError: datetime.datetime(2016, 12, 20, 22, 7, 36) is not JSON serializable.
parse error: Unfinished JSON term
$ cat sec.yaml
data:
    thing: 1
    thing2: "stuff"
    datething: 2016-12-20T22:07:36Z
    datethingstr: "2016-12-20T22:07:36Z"
```

this needs to override the default encoder class.
```
# isoformat
class DateTimeEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, o):
        if isinstance(o, datetime):
            return o.isoformat()
        return json.json.JSONEncoder.default(self, o)

# usage
json.dumps(..., cls=DateTimeEncoder)
```

issue_title: Read task tombstones from Celery database back-end, if configured
issue_body:
I'm using a database back-end for Celery so that I can have task tombstones for failure recovery purposes. I like Flower's default behavior of showing tasks that executed while it was running, but it's a bit misleading in my case since my Celery configuration establishes an expectation that a durable/all-inclusive store of task tombstones is available and visible in Flower.  Experimenting with Flower's `persistent` setting shows that it appears to be saving its own history to file -- duplicating data stored in my database back-end, and still maybe missing some tasks if Flower was down at any point when tasks were processed.

I can see how the file-based store is an advantage for Flower users who don't use/want a Celery database back-end, but ideally the database back-end should be used when it's configured. A nice future add-on might be to manage the database store via Flower (e.g. to delete task tombstones of a certain age).

issue_title: Pint doesn't seem to register the first alias of a unit definition
issue_body:
It seems to me that pint does not register the first alias when defining a unit. For example:
`teaspoon = tablespoon / 3 = tsp`
Will not register an alias with pint. But if i add another alias like so
`teaspoon = tablespoon / 3 = tsp = tsp`
It works fine. It will parse every alias after that first one.

issue_title: ERROR in SecretService priority(cls)
issue_body:
The availability checks in priority fail, because dbus_init() is called before the DISPLAY variable is checked for.

```
try:
  bus = secretstorage.dbus_init()
  secretstorage.Collection(bus)
except secretstorage.exceptions.SecretServiceNotAvailableException:
  raise RuntimeError("Unable to get initialize SecretService")
if 'DISPLAY' not in os.environ:
  raise RuntimeError("SecretService cannot run without a DISPLAY "
    "environment variable")
return 5
```

dbus_init() already fails with an uncaught DBusException if no DISPLAY variable is set. Pls pull the if-clause ahead. It already happens in 2.0 and I also found it in your repo's default branch.

It happens if no preference over backends is configured and when dbus is in general available, but simply no X11 display has been started.

Best, Manuel

issue_title: file keyrings config parser defaults to lower case option names: breaks unescaping
issue_body:
From the docs for RawConfigParser:

*All option names are passed through the optionxform() method. Its default implementation converts option names to lower case.*

This breaks the util.escape.unescape() method as the escapes are uppercase.

this can be fixed with:
```
#!python

config.optionxform = str
```
on the instance or by subclassing RawConfigParser.

Deprecate and rename the always_hash_content=False keyword

This is just a minor detail but the following keyword arg:
````py
sender = Sender(..., always_hash_content=False)
````
should be renamed to:
````py
sender = Sender(..., always_sign_content=False)
````
because the "hash" is just an artifact of signing the content. We can deprecate the old keyword arg and still support it.

Details: http://mohawk.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#skipping-content-checks

issue_body:
So, there's a file with format designed for ftp connections `$HOME/.netrc`. [Syntax info](https://stackoverflow.com/tags/.netrc/info).

```python
Link('foo', TypeRef['bar'], func, None)  # link to one
Link('foo', Optional[TypeRef['bar']], func, None)  # link to maybe one
Link('foo', Sequence[TypeRef['bar']], func, None)  # link to many

LinkQuery(TypeRef['bar'], SA_ENGINE, from_column=t.c.from_, to_column=t.c.to)
```
instead of
```python
Link('foo', One, func, edge='bar', requires=None)
Link('foo', Maybe, func, edge='bar', requires=None)
Link('foo', Many, func, edge='bar', requires=None)

LinkQuery(One, SA_ENGINE, edge='bar', from_column=t.c.from_, to_column=t.c.to)
```

issue_title: Process.open_files() on Linux might swallow AccessDenied
issue_body:
...that's because we pre emptively use os.path.islink() which might return False in case we don't have the permission to perform os.stat() against the designated path:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/ad2624ae1679e884087f448df8c1aedbaf3d346d/psutil/_pslinux.py#L1035
Instead we should just call os.readlink() directly and "continue" in case of EINVAL (meaning the path is not a link).

issue_title: OPTIONS requests not passed on
issue_body:
Not all OPTIONS requests are CORS preflight requests, but the middleware currently responds to all OPTIONS requests itself; it never passes an OPTIONS request on to the next app/middleware in the chain. This prevents me from writing a later filter that responds to non-CORS OPTIONS requests with an 'Allow:' header listing the request methods I support.

Firstly, would you accept a PR to fix this issue?

Also, should the code pass on all OPTIONS requests, then decorate them with CORS headers as appropriate?
Or should it decide whether the OPTIONS request is CORS or not, and handle the request itself if it's CORS, but pass it on if it's not CORS?

I don't understand whether an HTTP request can be both a CORS preflight request *and* an attempt to discover what request methods the server actually supports. Are these two possibilities exclusive?

In other words, would it be meaningful to respond with both an Access-Control-Allow-Methods header *and* an Allow header?

issue_title: Param objects could have ability to provide containers for multiple objects.
issue_body:
In some situations when we have `many=True` option on parameter provided it might be useful to provide a custom container type/class or method for grouping multiple instances.

Now every param class has only one method handler for single value param. Thanks to this implementation of parameter is short and consistent regardless of the `many` option setting. If multiple parameters are allowed then we always use list to contain provided parameters. Custom containers would be useful for additional preprocessing of params.

Here is example how this would work on real world example that allows joining multiple occurrences of `solrq.Q` object with specified operator ([solrq](https://github.com/swistakm/solrq) is example external package):

```python
from graceful.params import StringParam

import operator
from functools import reduce

class FilterQueryParam(StringParam):
    """
    Param that represents Solr filter queries logically 
   joined together depending on value of `op` argument
    """
    def __init__(
            self,
            details,
            solr_field,
            op=operator.iand,
            **kwargs
    ):
        if solr_field is None:
            raise ValueError("{} needs a `field` param cannot be None".format(
                self.__class__.__name__)
            )

        self.solr_field = solr_field
        self.op = op

        super(FilterQueryParam, self).__init__(
            details, **kwargs
        )

    def value(self, raw_value):
        return Q({self.solr_field: raw_value})

    def container(self, values):
        return reduce(self.op, values) if len(values) > 1 else values[0]
```

With such API whenever `many` is set to true then `.container()` will be fed with list of values got from multiple calls to `.value()` method handler. Additionally there can be an additional `container_class`  keyword argument stored as Param attribute on initialisation (default `container=list`) that will be used by default implementation of container method:

```python

class BaseParam(object):
    ...

    def container(self, values):
       return self.container_class(values)
```   

In this way this feature can be used even on existing basic Param classes without the need of creating custom param definitions.

issue_title: Add German support for typewrite()
issue_body:
From a user's email:

I use PyAutoGUI-0.9.33-py3.4.egg-info in one of my programs. When I use the function pyautogui.typewrite special characters like ö,ä,ü,#,@ ß, do not work.

I found an workaround for me on the following side:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13289777/how-can-i-send-keyboard-commands-hold-release-simultanous-with-a-python-script


Maybe you can use this code for windows, so special characters are also supported if it not already work now.

 

Thank you for your time and thanks for PyAutoGUI :)


Following the code that works for me:


import ctypes
import time

SendInput = ctypes.windll.user32.SendInput

PUL = ctypes.POINTER(ctypes.c_ulong)
class KeyBdInput(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("wVk", ctypes.c_ushort),
                ("wScan", ctypes.c_ushort),
                ("dwFlags", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("time", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("dwExtraInfo", PUL)]

class HardwareInput(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("uMsg", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("wParamL", ctypes.c_short),
                ("wParamH", ctypes.c_ushort)]

class MouseInput(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("dx", ctypes.c_long),
                ("dy", ctypes.c_long),
                ("mouseData", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("dwFlags", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("time",ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("dwExtraInfo", PUL)]

class Input_I(ctypes.Union):
    _fields_ = [("ki", KeyBdInput),
                 ("mi", MouseInput),
                 ("hi", HardwareInput)]

class Input(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("type", ctypes.c_ulong),
                ("ii", Input_I)]


def writetext(text_unicode):
        def PressKey_local(KeyUnicode):
            extra = ctypes.c_ulong(0)
            ii_ = Input_I()
            ii_.ki = KeyBdInput( 0, KeyUnicode, KEYEVENTF_UNICODE, 0, ctypes.pointer(extra) )
            x = Input( ctypes.c_ulong(1), ii_ )
            ctypes.windll.user32.SendInput(1, ctypes.pointer(x), ctypes.sizeof(x))
        def ReleaseKey_local(KeyUnicode):
            extra = ctypes.c_ulong(0)
            ii_ = Input_I()
            ii_.ki = KeyBdInput( 0, KeyUnicode, KEYEVENTF_UNICODE|KEYEVENTF_KEYUP, 0, ctypes.pointer(extra) )
            x = Input( ctypes.c_ulong(1), ii_ )
            ctypes.windll.user32.SendInput(1, ctypes.pointer(x), ctypes.sizeof(x))   
        for c in text_unicode:
            PressKey_local(ord(c))
            ReleaseKey_local(ord(c))

writetext("Ölgemälde")

issue_title: Don't use len() in the virtual IO setup?
issue_body:
Currently, we have this:

```Python
def vio_get_filelen(user_data):
    # first try __len__(), if not available fall back to seek()/tell()
    try:
        size = len(file)
    except TypeError:
        curr = file.tell()
        file.seek(0, SEEK_END)
        size = file.tell()
        file.seek(curr, SEEK_SET)
    return size
```

I suspect that the use of `len()` is a now obsolete artifact from the development in #1.
It was added there to seemingly support "streaming", but it turned out that this is not supported by the virtual IO call anyway.

Every supported file-like object *must have* the methods `seek()` and `tell()` anyway, so I think its superfluous to check with `len()` first.

Can we get rid of this remnant from the past?

I guess the cleaned-up version would look like this:

```Python
def vio_get_filelen(user_data):
    curr = file.tell()
    file.seek(0, SEEK_END)
    size = file.tell()
    file.seek(curr, SEEK_SET)
    return size
```

I'm wondering why `get_filelen` is even part of libsndfile's API, since it can be easily implemented with `seek` and `tell` ...?

issue_title: Where is HAVE_RRD_GRAPH defined?
issue_body:
I have Lubuntu 15.04, with `librrd-dev` package already installed.

I used a Python 2.7 virtualenv and then tried installing this module using `pip install rrdtool`. The resulting module, however, did not have `graph` support.

I then manually cloned the repository, manually edited the C source file to include `#define HAVE_RRD_GRAPH`, and then used `setup.py` to build and install it in the virtualenv. The module worked fine afterwards, WITH `graph` support.

So… Where is `HAVE_RRD_GRAPH` defined? After a quick look, I believe it gets defined only in `rrd_config.h`, which is only available during the compilation of `rrdtool`, which means this C definition is not exported anywhere. If this is the case, maybe we should open an issue at https://github.com/oetiker/rrdtool-1.x asking to expose thise definition. Alternatively, we can work around it in this module source code or in this module build logic.

issue_title: UInput.from_device does not merge every event code
issue_body:
When using `UInput.from_device()` to merge the capabilities of several devices, the resulting capabilities of the uinput device are not the full list of those in the source devices.

Since for each ev_type in `dev.capabilities()` we have a list of ev_codes, if two devices have same ev_type but differente ev_codes, the update method on `uinput.py` replaces the previous list with the last ones:
```
     all_capabilities = {}
     for dev in device_instances:
         all_capabilities.update(dev.capabilities())
```

To correctly merge capabilities of several devices we should do something like this to extend the lists instead of replacing them:
```  
    all_capabilities = defaultdict(list)
    for dev in device_instances:
        dev_caps = dev.capabilities()        
        for ev_type, ev_codes in dev_caps.iteritems():
            all_capabilities[ev_type].extend(ev_codes)
            all_capabilities[ev_type] = list(set(all_capabilities[ev_type]))  # removes duplicate ev_codes
```  

The last line could be avoided if instead of lists we had sets for device capabilities, which would automatically avoid duplicate ev_codes, but that might be a bigger API change, I think.

I could do a pull request if you find it appropriate

```
# Imports
import nltk.corpus
import nltk.tokenize.punkt
import nltk.stem.snowball
from nltk.corpus import wordnet
import string
from chatterbot.adapters import Adapter

class LogicAdapter(Adapter):
    """
    This is an abstract class that represents the interface
    that all logic adapters should implement.
    """

    def can_process(self, statement):
        """
        A preliminary check that is called to determine if a
        logic adapter can process a given statement. By default,
        this method returns true but it can be overridden in
        child classes as needed.
        """
        return True

    def process(self, statement):
        # """
        # Method that takes an input statement and returns
        # a confidence value and a statement as output.
        # """

        # raise self.AdapterMethodNotImplementedError()
        # Get default English stopwords and extend with punctuation
        stopwords = nltk.corpus.stopwords.words('english')
        stopwords.extend(string.punctuation)
        stopwords.append('')

        def get_wordnet_pos(pos_tag):
            if pos_tag[1].startswith('J'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.ADJ)
            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('V'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.VERB)
            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('N'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.NOUN)
            elif pos_tag[1].startswith('R'):
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.ADV)
            else:
                return (pos_tag[0], wordnet.NOUN)

        # Create tokenizer and stemmer
        # tokenizer = nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize()
        lemmatizer = nltk.stem.wordnet.WordNetLemmatizer()

        def is_ci_lemma_stopword_set_match(a, b, threshold=0.5):
            """Check if a and b are matches."""
            pos_a = map(get_wordnet_pos, nltk.pos_tag(tokenizer.nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize(a)))
            pos_b = map(get_wordnet_pos, nltk.pos_tag(tokenizer.nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize(b)))
            lemmae_a = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token.lower().strip(string.punctuation), pos) for token, pos in pos_a \
                            if pos == wordnet.NOUN and token.lower().strip(string.punctuation) not in stopwords]
            lemmae_b = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token.lower().strip(string.punctuation), pos) for token, pos in pos_b \
                            if pos == wordnet.NOUN and token.lower().strip(string.punctuation) not in stopwords]

            # Calculate Jaccard similarity
            ratio = len(set(lemmae_a).intersection(lemmae_b)) / float(len(set(lemmae_a).union(lemmae_b)))
            return (ratio >= threshold)
        def get(self, input_statement):
            """
            Takes a statement string and a list of statement strings.
            Returns the approximate matching statement from the list.
            """
            statement_list = self.context.storage.get_response_statements()
            print(statement_list)
            if not statement_list:
                if self.has_storage_context:
                    # Use a randomly picked statement
                    self.logger.info(
                        u'No statements have known responses. ' +
                        u'Choosing a random response to return.'
                        )
                    return 0, self.context.storage.get_random()
                else:
                    raise self.EmptyDatasetException()

            confidence = -1
            closest_match = input_statement
            # Find the closest matching known statement
            for statement in statement_list:
                print("statement", statement.text);
                # ratio = fuzz.ratio(input_statement.text, statement.text)
                ratio = is_ci_lemma_stopword_set_match(input_statement.text, statement.text)
                if ratio > confidence:
                    confidence = ratio
                    closest_match = statement
                    # Convert the confidence integer to a percent
                confidence /= 100.0
                return confidence, closest_match

    class EmptyDatasetException(Exception):

        def __init__(self, value="An empty set was received when at least one statement was expected."):
            self.value = value

        def __str__(self):
            return repr(self.value)

```

Although I really like this prompt, I still prefer bash-git-prompt (https://github.com/magicmonty/bash-git-prompt) when working in git repos.
So I configured bash-git-prompt to be active only in git repos (GIT_PROMPT_ONLY_IN_REPO=1).
Unfortunately, powerline-shell does not update the current path then leaving a git repo. It shows the old path at first. After some action on the prompt, the path updates itself.

_Example_ (entering git repo (activating bash-git-prompt), then cd back, followed by enter to update the old path):
```
 user  servername  ~  $  cd /path/to/git/repo/
✔  /path/to/git/repo/ [master|✚ 23]
10:52 $ cd
 user  servername  path  to  git  repo  $ 
 user  servername  ~  $ 
```

Without powerline-shell, the (default) prompt is updated correctly at once after leaving a git repo.

issue_title: Error when deploying to Heroku
issue_body:
I was having this error when trying to deploy a Django app to Heroku (following their tutorial). This also happens when running ```foreman start``` locally.

```
ImproperlyConfigured: Requested setting USE_I18N, but settings are not configured.
You must either define the environment variable DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE or call
settings.configure() before accessing settings.
```

The solution?
Setting the ```DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE``` *before* importing and calling Cling.

```
import os
from django.core.wsgi import get_wsgi_application

os.environ.setdefault('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE', 'trappt.settings')

from dj_static import Cling
application = Cling(get_wsgi_application())
```

issue_title: FIPS 186-3 DSA parameters
issue_body:
our current FIPS standard for DSA keys allows for (p,q) pairs of:

L = 1024, N = 160
L = 2048, N = 224
L = 2048, N = 256
L = 3072, N = 256

SSHKeys currently enforces 1024 bits which isn't accurate. The vast majority of SSH keys are created with ssh-keygen which enforces 1024 bits for DSA keys, however, keys are easily made with other software and as long as the math is correct, the key will work with SSH

issue_title: Theoretical deadlock situation
issue_body:
Here is current release Lua script:
```lua
if redis.call("get", KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
    redis.call("del", KEYS[2])
    redis.call("lpush", KEYS[2], 1)
    return redis.call("del", KEYS[1])
else
    return 0
end
```

And here is acquire Python code:
```python
while busy:
    busy = not self._client.set(self._name, self._id, nx=True, ex=self._expire)
    if busy:
        if timed_out:
            return False
        elif blocking:
            timed_out = not self._client.blpop(self._signal, blpop_timeout)
        else:
            logger.debug("Failed to get %r.", self._name)
            return False
```

Imagine such situation, with two workers:
- The first one acquired the lock previously.
- The second is blocked by `blpop()` call and waiting. 
- Then worker-1 releases the lock, and Redis starts executing the Lua script and execution point comes to `LPUSH` and Redis executes `LPUSH`. 
- After that worker-2 wakes up, tries to call `SET NX`, which doesn't succeeds, so worker-2 calls `BLPOP` again and becomes blocked. 
- Then Redis continues execution of Lua script launched by worker-1 and calls `DEL` on lock's key, but it doesn't affects worker-2. 

In the result - worker-2 is blocked forever.

issue_title: Documentation correction: need to use io.open()
issue_body:
The README right now says to open the file with:

    with TextIOWrapper(open(filename, 'rb'), encoding='utf-8') as f:

This works in Python 3, but not Python 2.  TextIOWrapper needs a "stream", not just a "file".  In Python 3, `open` is the same thing as `io.open`, and it returns a stream.  In Python 2, `io.open` returns a stream, but `open` returns a file.

If you try to run this as written in Python 2, you get an error (no "readable" attribute).

The simple fix is to use `io.open` instead of `open` (for both the reading and writing cases), and it works in both Python 2 and 3.

issue_title: Make unversioned check failures more understandable
issue_body:
There are three situations when a check fails against a playbook (unversioned playbooks should be treated as playbooks on very latest version)

1. check version > playbook version. Should warn that the playbook doesn't pass a "Future Standard"
2. check version <= playbook version. Should error that the playbook doesn't pass a Standard
3. unversioned check. Should warn that the playbook doesn't pass a "Best practice"

Is there a way to add weights to individual observation in KaplanMeierFitter().fit()? This is useful for inverse probability censor weights.

issue_title: Should KaplanMeierFitter have a `strata` option?
issue_body:
A common task with KM is to plot many survival functions on the same plot. Lifelines atm kind of makes this difficult, something like:

```python
ax = subplot(111)

for value in df['cata'].unique():
    ix = df['cata'] == value
    kmf.fit(df.ix[ix]['T'], df.ix[ix]['E'], label=value)
    ax = kmf.plot(ax=ax)
```

It's not obvious to do this the first time you encounter lifelines. A proposal may be an additional arg in KMFitter, `strata`, that does something like this all for you:

```python
kmf.fit(df['T'], df['E'], strata=df['cata'])
```

Then, `kmf.survival_function_` returns a dataframe of survival functions (`median_` is the same). `plot` is definetly easier for the user, but internally may be more complicated. 

Other options:

1. Better docs to demonstrate how this is done.

2. A new Fitter that handles this

3. Helper functions to make this easier.

````
issue_title: IOError on sending PONG
issue_body:
We're getting quite a lot of these errors (about 2k/month): https://sentry.io/share/issue/06b32b9f853a4d4abc4f664a0734347a/

```
OSError: Socket is closed
  File "engineio/socket.py", line 210, in _websocket_handler
    self.receive(pkt)
  File "engineio/socket.py", line 54, in receive
    self.send(packet.Packet(packet.PONG, pkt.data))
  File "engineio/socket.py", line 68, in send
    raise IOError('Socket is closed')
```

It looks like this happens when the socket is closed between the client PING and the server PONG. I'm not 100% why this happens, I'd say that the clients disconnect in between PING and PONG and thus that error is thrown. 

I'd say that the solution here is to ignore it for PONG in particular since it's clear that it's not possible to send a reply which is the whole point of this PONG - but this is only based on my assumption that it is caused by client disconnects.

issue_title: Case-sensitivity in URLs
issue_body:
It looks like the URLs created from sass + less are being created as something like, `.../Roboto/Roboto-Light.woff2`, instead of `.../roboto/Roboto-Light.woff2`. On a case-sensitive filesystem (Linux, in my case), this means that the files can't be found.

Any thoughts on the best way to address this? A few options I thought of:

1. Rename `roboto` to `Roboto`, but I fear that would break other things.
1. Use sass's `to-lower-case` function, but less lacks a similar function.
1. Add an extra, properly-cased arg to the mixin, but that's just a lot of repeated crap.

issue_title: Equal phonenumbers not equal
issue_body:
When you create two records with phonenumberfield, one with '+44 113 8921113' and other with '+441138921113' value and than compare them, they are not equal (```comp_before``` is ```False```).

```python
class MandatoryPhoneNumber(models.Model):
    phone_number = PhoneNumberField()

first = MandatoryPhoneNumber.objects.create(phone_number='+44 113 8921113')
second = MandatoryPhoneNumber.objects.create(phone_number='+441138921113')

comp_before = first.phone_number == second.phone_number

first.save()
second.save()

first_db = MandatoryPhoneNumber.objects.get(id=first.id)
second_db = MandatoryPhoneNumber.objects.get(id=second.id)

comp_after = first_db.phone_number == second_db.phone_number
```

This is because parent class for ```PhoneNumberField``` compares in ```__eq__``` function every attribute of phonenumber and for the '+44 113 8921113' and '+441138921113' differs ```raw_input``` attribute.

But when you save these two records to database and than read them from database, these two records are equal (```comp_after``` is ```True```), because we store phone number in database as string with constant format and initialize from database provided "normalized" string format.

And this is inconsistency - ```comp_before != comp_after```. It must be ```comp_before == comp_after```

issue_title: Non-model serializers always include method fields
issue_body:
Non-model serializer, which don't inherit their fields using the `Meta.fields` iterable, automatically take the fields as defined on the class. This means that all `SerializerMethodField`s are included, whether the user intends to expand them or not.

For non-model fields, we'll need to pass over the field definitions, and if not expanded, attempt to pop the field from the list.

issue_title: json.dumps not consistently setting cls kwarg to supplied encoder
issue_body:
All versions of python-cloudant and Python are affected.

We specifically allow for setting an `encoder` via key word argument when constructing a `client` instance.  The assumption is that this encoder would be set for all JSON encoding handled by the library, in other words all instances of `json.dumps` in the code should have a `cls` setting pointing to the encoder set on the client.  However, the use of the `encoder` is being used in some instances and not others.

We should fix this and update all occurrences of `json.dumps` to have the `cls` keyword argument set to the client encoder.

issue_title: Reuse Variable but with different attributes
issue_body:
Sometimes we need to share weights in network. And Its' advised in mxnet to reuse variable by applyinng  one weight and referencing multiple times. That's OK when variable attributes like lr_mult wd_mult is fixed.
But what if we want to change the learning rate in different network structure but still share the same weight.

My solution is to manually call symbol.set_attr with different learning_rate before different module initialization, is that OK? do you have a simpler way. Is that OK to define multiple variables with same name in this case? Thanks.

issue_title: Hierarchy.name or base_url unique?
issue_body: In UELC, at least, it would be simpler if Hierarchy.base_url had `unique=True`. The back-end shouldn't allow users to clone multiple hierarchies at the same path.

issue_title: watchman writes incorrect path to com.github.facebook.watchman.plist file when installing via brew
issue_body:
When installing watchman via brew with

`brew install watchman --HEAD`, it writes

`<string>/usr/local/Cellar/watchman/HEAD/libexec/bin/watchman</string>` to the `com.github.facebook.watchman.plist` file

while in fact, the real location is something like

`<string>/usr/local/Cellar/watchman/HEAD-39b814a/libexec/bin/watchman</string>`

This leads to watchman hanging indefinitely on OSX with an error like

`launchd checkin failed: No such process` in the watchman logs

issue_title: Language model incorrectly drops spaces for out-of-vocabulary words
issue_body:
Mozilla DeepSpeech will sometimes create long runs of text with no spaces:

```
omiokaarforfthelastquarterwastoget
```

This happens even with short audio clips (4 seconds) with a native American english speaker recorded using a high quality microphone in Mac OS X laptops. I've isolated the problem to interaction with the language model rather than the acoustic model or length of audio clips, as the problem goes away when the language model is turned off.

The problem might be related to encountering out-of-vocabulary terms.

I’ve put together test files with results that show the issue is related to the language model somehow rather than the length of the audio or the acoustic model.

I’ve provided 10 chunked WAV files at 16khz 16 bit depth, each 4 seconds long, that are a subset of a fuller 15 minute audio file (I have not provided that full 15 minute file, as a few shorter reproducible chunks are sufficient to reproduce the problem):

https://www.dropbox.com/sh/3qy65r6wo8ldtvi/AAAAVinsD_kcCi8Bs6l3zOWFa?dl=0

The audio segments deliberately include occasional out-of-vocabulary terms, mostly technical, such as “OKR”, “EdgeStore”, “CAPE”, etc.

Also in that folder are several text files that show the output with the standard language model being used, showing the garbled words together (`chunks_with_language_model.txt`):

```
Running inference for chunk 1
so were trying again a maybeialstart this time

Running inference for chunk 2
omiokaarforfthelastquarterwastoget

Running inference for chunk 3
to car to state deloedmarchinstrumnalha

Running inference for chunk 4
a tonproductcaseregaugesomd produce sidnelfromthat

Running inference for chunk 5
i am a to do that you know 

Running inference for chunk 6
we finish the kepehandlerrwend finished backfileprocessing 

Running inference for chunk 7
and is he teckdatthatwewould need to do to split the cape 

Running inference for chunk 8
out from sir handler and i are on new 

Running inference for chunk 9
he is not monolithic am andthanducotingswrat 

Running inference for chunk 10
relizationutenpling paws on that until it its a product signal
```

Then, I’ve provided similar output with the language model turned off (`chunks_without_language_model.txt`):

```
Running inference for chunk 1
so we're tryng again ah maybe alstart this time

Running inference for chunk 2
omiokaar forf the last quarter was to get

Running inference for chunk 3
oto car to state deloed march in strumn alha

Running inference for chunk 4
um ton product  caser egauges somd produc sidnel from that

Running inference for chunk 5
am ah to do that ou nowith

Running inference for chunk 6
we finishd the kepe handlerr wend finished backfile processinga

Running inference for chunk 7
on es eteckdat that we would need to do to split the kae ha

Running inference for chunk 8
rout frome sir hanler and ik ar on newh

Running inference for chunk 9
ch las not monoliic am andthan ducotings wrat 

Running inference for chunk 10
relization u en pling a pas on that until it its a product signal
```

I’ve included both these files in the shared Dropbox folder link above.

Here’s what the correct transcript should be, manually done (`chunks_correct_manual_transcription.txt`):

```
So, we're trying again, maybe I'll start this time.

So my OKR for the last quarter was to get AutoOCR to a state that we could
launch an external alpha, and product could sort of gauge some product signal
from that. To do that we finished the CAPE handler, we finished backfill 
processing, we have some tech debt that we would need to do to split the CAPE 
handler out from the search handler and make our own new handler so its not
monolithic, and do some things around CAPE utilization. We are kind of putting
a pause on that until we get some product signal.
This shows the language model is the source of this problem; I’ve seen anecdotal reports from this message base and blog posts that this is a wide spread problem. Perhaps when the language model hits an unknown n-gram, it ends up combining all of them together rather than retaining the space between them.
```

This shows the language model is the source of this problem; I’ve seen anecdotal reports from the official message base and blog posts that this is a wide spread problem. Perhaps when the language model hits an unknown n-gram, it ends up combining all of them together rather than retaining the space between them.

Discussion around this bug started on the standard DeepSpeech discussion forum:
https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/text-produced-has-long-strings-of-words-with-no-spaces/24089/13
https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/longer-audio-files-with-deep-speech/22784/3

- **Have I written custom code (as opposed to running examples on an unmodified clone of the repository)**:

The standard `client.py` was slightly modified to segment the longer 15 minute audio clip into 4 second blocks.

- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**:

Mac OS X 10.12.6 (16G1036)

- **TensorFlow installed from (our builds, or upstream TensorFlow)**:

Both Mozilla DeepSpeech and TensorFlow were installed into a virtualenv setup via the following requirements.txt file:

```
tensorflow==1.4.0
deepspeech==0.1.0
numpy==1.13.3
scipy==0.19.1
webrtcvad==2.0.10
```

- **TensorFlow version (use command below)**:

```
('v1.4.0-rc1-11-g130a514', '1.4.0')
```

- **Python version**: 

```
Python 2.7.13
```

- **Bazel version (if compiling from source)**:

Did not compile from source.

- **GCC/Compiler version (if compiling from source)**:

Same

- **CUDA/cuDNN version**:

Used CPU only version

- **GPU model and memory**:

Used CPU only version

- **Exact command to reproduce**:

I haven't provided my full modified `client.py` that segments longer audio, but to run with a language model using the standard `deepspeech` command against a known 4 seconds audio clip included in the Dropbox folder shared above you can run the following:

```
# Set $DEEPSPEECH to where full Deep Speech checkout is; note that my own git checkout
# for the `deepspeech` runner is at git sha fef25e9ea6b0b6d96dceb610f96a40f2757e05e4
deepspeech $DEEPSPEECH/models/output_graph.pb chunk_2_length_4.0_s.wav $DEEPSPEECH/models/alphabet.txt $DEEPSPEECH/models/lm.binary $DEEPSPEECH/models/trie

# Similar command to run without language model -- spaces retained for unknown words:
deepspeech $DEEPSPEECH/models/output_graph.pb chunk_2_length_4.0_s.wav $DEEPSPEECH/models/alphabet.txt 
```

This is clearly a bug and not a feature :)

issue_title: Don't log histograms of variables by default
issue_body: Histogram summaries are expensive, we should find out if logging them by default is a performance problem, and disable them if so.

issue_title: Variables are not restored from a checkpoint file
issue_body:
I am trying to use a checkpoint file to save and restore variables.
But restoring doesn't work while saving works.

`python3 DeepSpeech.py --train_files "data/mydata/train.csv" --dev_files "data/mydata/dev.csv" --test_files "data/mydata/test.csv" --train_batch_size 12 --dev_batch_size 12 --test_batch_size 12 --display_step 1 --validation_step 1 --epoch 15 --checkpoint_dir "data/mydata/checkpoint“ --log_level 0`

Checkpoint files are created every 10 minutes (default).
But when I stop the training and run again, it starts the training from the beginning again. (From batch step 0, epoch 0)

I added `checkpoint_dir = FLAGS.checkpoint_dir` to the argument of MonitoredTrainingSession, and the model was restored properly.
I wonder if this is the right thing, or is there another correct way?

issue_title: "Too Many Files Open" Error - Files Not Being Closed?
issue_body:
I've been using the ANNOY Python API to build and save several index tables in order to load and reuse subsets of these in another script (at most about 400 in a subset at a time). The library is wonderfully fast, but I've been encountering "too many files open" errors after some time. I'm loading each index once, doing stuff with it, then unloading it before the loop continues onwards with the next index table.

Here is a partial code snippet that reproduces the error by reloading the same index table (510 x 64) over and over (run the loop multiple times if the error doesn't show up immediately):

	from annoy import AnnoyIndex
	index_table = AnnoyIndex( 64,metric='angular' )
	for i in xrange(10000):
		index_table.load( index_table_filename )
		print i  # just seeing when it failed
		index_table.unload( index_table_filename )

After some sleuthing into the GitHub source code, I notice that unload() unmaps the file from memory, but doesn't close the file opened using load(). Would it be possible to get file closing functionality incorporated into the Python wrapper somehow please? Or am I missing something vital in using ANNOY?

Thanks for all the hard work on the API!

So I was hoping to be able to be able to write:

for dataset in datasets:

issue_title: Cache outputs generated by the WebUI
issue_body:
Exporting outputs via the WebUI is an expensive operation: i.e. an hmap for a national wide computation takes, on Wilson, 10 minutes.

With the current workflow the output is dumped to `/tmp` and then sent via HTTP.
Files in `/tmp` are created with a random name, so a new export is always performed even if a copy of it already exists on the filesystem. This is very inefficient from both the CPU and disk space usage.

We should generate outputs with a known name and before asking the engine to export an output we can check if the file already exists on the filesystem.

I don't see issues with cache invalidation, in the sense that if we use a combination of ID and checksum we can be sure that the output on fs is always the right one (once a calculation is complete it should be immutable).

issue_title: Multipart upload holds onto data in memory
issue_body:
I am trying to upload large files to s3 under tight memory constraints (~2GB files with <1GB memory) and doing a multipart upload is crashing my application due to memory usage.

I've isolated the issue to the fact that even though the `upload_part` request is executed synchronously, the data is still being held in memory while more uploads are being performed, which causes memory usage to accumulate.

Is there a way to do a multipart upload where i can be sure boto will only hold onto one part at a time?

issue_title: VimSessionOrientedStub - makeUserLoginMethod
issue_body:
Hi, new to pyVmomi, Python, VMWare, etc. :-)   Tasked with moving existing pySphere code to pyVmomi.  We are registering an extension with the extension manager with pySphere (Storing certificate, then logging in later via certificate).  I'd like to do the same with pyVmomi but I'm having some difficulty.  Searching yielded that I'll need to use the makeExtensionLoginMethod(), however, I could not get this to 'work', so as a sanity check and baseline, I tried makeUserLoginMethod() as follows.

```
stub = SoapStubAdapter(host='<fqdn>', ns="vim25/5.5")
sessionStub = VimSessionOrientedStub(stub, VimSessionOrientedStub.makeUserLoginMethod(user, password), 1)
si = vim.ServiceInstance("ServiceInstance", sessionStub)
si.content.sessionManager.sessionList
```

However, this also fails for me.  I traced my issue into the _doLogin() of makeUserLoginMethod().  
```
si = vim.ServiceInstance("ServiceInstance", soapStub)
sm = si.content.sessionManager --> Throws exception

```
si.content.sessionManager seems to throw an exception that _CallLoginMethod() traps in the finally clause.  I don't have much more information than that at the moment. 

My ultimate goal is to get makeExtensionLoginMethod() working, but I'd like to first see makeUserLoginMethod() work as a sanity check.  Any advice, pointers, etc. would be greatly appreciated.   I'm going to dig into this more as I'm adjusting to Python (just learned about pdb), but wanted to get this posted to see if anyone had any ideas. 

Thanks!

issue_title: Property Collector: Getting the folder name of a VM
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm using the property collector to get all of the VMs in the VC's inventory.
I am using the code in the example here: https://github.com/vmware/pyvmomi-community-samples/blob/master/samples/vminfo_quick.py

Basically, my FilterSpec is:
ObjectView uses a TraversalSpec for a ContainerView tracking all vim.VirtualMachine instances recrusively starting from the root folder.
There is one PropertySpec  for vim.VirtualMachine

For collecting value types in the VirtualMachine object it works perfectly. But when I try collecting the VM's parent.name  which is the name property of a Folder managed object it throws an InvalidProperty error.

I've tried adding to the selectSet of the ContainerView TraversalSpec, a new TraversalSpec for vim.VirtualMachine with path="parent" and skip=False. Additionally, I've added a PropertySpec for vim.Folder's name property.

This worked but i've got dicts containing the VM properties and after all of the VM dicts, a lot of dicts containing a name of a folder.

What I want is to get the properties of the VM and the name of it's parent folder in a single dict (in a single result)
How can I do that?

Thanks for the helpers :)

Cordially

issue_title: Failed. Response status: 500. Response message: Internal Server Error
issue_body:
### General Information - Mode (Sandbox/Live): Live - PayPal-Debug-ID(s) (from any logs): bac7577845936 - Version of Python used: 2.7 - SDK Version: 1.7.2 ### Issue Description > Please include as many details (logs, steps to reproduce, frameworks/dependencies) as you can to help us reproduce this issue faster. Hello guys, recently we faced a problem with making payouts through paypalrestsdk. In some cases we receive an error (listed below) while execution method `.create(sync_mode=True)` and decline current payout on our side, assuming you didn't do it on your side. issue_title: Windows with_rw-directory TC helper fails to delete repos
issue_body:
On Windows, `os.rmtree` cannot not remove directories with read-only files; such are the git db directories.

The fix is something like this:

```python
           ...
        finally:
            # Need to collect here to be sure all handles have been closed. It appears
            # a windows-only issue. In fact things should be deleted, as well as
            # memory maps closed, once objects go out of scope. For some reason
            # though this is not the case here unless we collect explicitly.
            if not keep:
                gc.collect()

                # From http://stackoverflow.com/a/4829285/548792
                def on_rm_error(func, path, exc_info):
                    import stat
                    os.chmod(path, stat.S_IWRITE)
                    func(path)

                shutil.rmtree(path, onerror=on_rm_error)
````

issue_title: ykman fails to connect to YubiKey when OATH code is being generated with the yubico-authenticator
issue_body:
### Version information

`macOS Sierra 10.12.6`

```
$ ykman --version
YubiKey Manager (ykman) version: 0.4.5
Libraries:
    libykpers 1.18.0
    libu2f-host 1.1.4
    libusb 1.0.21

$ ykman info
Device name: YubiKey 4
Serial number: 4039699
Firmware version: 4.2.7
Enabled connection(s): OTP+U2F+CCID

Device capabilities:
    OTP:	Enabled
    U2F:	Enabled
    CCID:	Enabled
    OPGP:	Enabled
    PIV:	Enabled
    OATH:	Enabled
```

### Steps to reproduce

- In yubico-authenticator, right-click on one of the slots and select "Generate code".
- Verify that yubico-authenticator prompts "Touch your YubiKey to generate the code.".
- In a terminal, run `ykman oath code` (or `ykman oath list`).
- Verify that `ykman` is waiting. (doesn't return instantly)
- Touch your YubiKey in response to the original yubico-authenticator request.
- Verify that yubico-authenticator successfully generates an OATH code.
- Observe that `ykman` **fails** with the following:
```
$ ykman oath code
Usage: ykman [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

Error: Failed connecting to the YubiKey.
```

Interestingly, if you use `ykman` itself to generate the OATH codes, (by running `ykman oath code <slot name>`) both operations succeed.

issue_title: Expiry field on resources
issue_body:
In the world of automation, no looking back, moving fast and breaking things it greatly simplify extra work to have an expiry field on resources. This is a datetime field that renders a resource as either orphaned and easily reportable, hidden and ready to overwrite, or deleted - I mostly lean toward the former.

An example use case would be running a periodic job to push updates to NSoT about a resource, either from local server or EC2 or anything, that gets decommed suddenly and letting it be cleaned up nicely.

issue_title: Is sortable_field_name required?
issue_body:
Wanting to have nested inlines in the admin (without sorting), I tried the app with a string value for `OPTIONS['string_if_invalid']` in `settings.TEMPLATES`. I encountered two errors:

- Without assigning a `sortable_field_name` to the inline, a `KeyError` was raised, because the `string_if_invalid` value is assumed to be a field of the `ModelForm` used, which was not the case.

- I therefore set `sortable_field_name` to `None` (assuming it would disable sorting). In this case, the `string_if_invalid` value was displayed in the HTML and the formset was not working at all.

Without setting `string_if_invalid'`, the app works, but still I think this is not an expected behavior, unless `sortable_field_name` is required, which I did not see mentioned in the docs (perhaps I missed it?).

issue_title: login_with_public_key login successfully using invalid keyfile
issue_body:
Currently login_with_public_key  will still pass even if you provide an invalid keyfile as long as you provide the correct user password in the 'password' parameter. According to documentation, 'password' parameter should act as a 'passphrase' for the keyfile if it is needed. Current implementation seems to default to normal log in when invalid keyfile is provided. 
I think 'password' should no longer be provided using this keyword since users really intend to login using their provided keyfile and should fail if this is invalid. 'password' argument should also be replaced by 'passphrase' to avoid confusion for provided 'passphrase' for the keyfile.

Since changing the shell fixes the issue, I'm not really sure it's pipenv problem, but since it broke with the pipenv update, I think we need at least investigate it.

issue_body:
##### Issue description

`pipenv shell` doesn't work using fish.

The last [two comments in this issue](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/2340) are related. I tried the recommended commands, and they do work:
- `env SHELL=/usr/bin/fish pipenv shell` works
- `pipenv shell --fancy` also works

I'm using fish version 2.7.1

##### Expected result

Pipenv should source `/home/justin/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate.fish”` and enter a shell in the virtual environment.

##### Actual result

Pipenv sources `/home/justin/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate”`. I'm left in the subshell with python still pointing at the system python installation.

##### Steps to replicate

```
$ pipenv install
$ pipenv shell
  . /home/justin/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate
~/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate (line 23): Unsupported use of '||'. In fish, please use 'COMMAND; or COMMAND'.
    if [ -n "${BASH-}" ] || [ -n "${ZSH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
                          ^
from sourcing file ~/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate
	called on line 230 of file /usr/share/fish/config.fish

in function “.”
	called on standard input

source: Error while reading file “/home/justin/.local/share/virtualenvs/project-GA1fLWru/bin/activate”
$ which python
/usr/bin/python
```

issue_title: entry_point with un-satisfied extras_require doesn't throw a DistributionNotFound if you installed your package as a wheel
Stall streaming connections
issue_body:
In your documentation you recommend that the client application should terminate the connection and re-connects its corresponding stream in the event of:
- No data has been received (no ticks, no heartbeats) from the rates stream for more than 10 seconds.
- No data has been received (no events, no heartbeats) from the events stream for more than 20 seconds.

None of these recommendations are implemented in v20 python wrapper. There's no way to set `v20.Content` timeout nor there is a way to catch stall connection. 

Please fix this.

issue_title: Resolve references when inheriting docstrings
issue_body:
Related to #156, but different--since automatic docstring inheritance isn't a normal "thing" in Python, Sphinx falls over a bit when this happens.

Normally when we write a docstring we don't have to write references *within the same module* with a fully qualified name.  For example, if I'm writing a docstring for a method in the `ConfigItem` class, I can refer to it just by:

```
`ConfigItem`
```

However, if I have a subclass of `ConfigItem` in a different module, and it inherits a docstring from one of `ConfigItem`'s methods that it otherwise overrides, it can no longer resolve that reference, because it looks for `ConfigItem` within the same module, not within the module that docstring originally came from.  So we need to make autodoc somehow aware that this *can* happen in some cases.

issue_title: non-ideal error message for max_errors hit
issue_body:
## System

* master branch (including latest fix to returncode), Py27, Linux

## Summary

* In my specific run, the same error comes up twice in a row (EDDRM)
* The second time it happens, custodian says that it hits max_errors (I think this part is OK) and raises a custodian error to exit.
* What happens next is that this results in a non-zero return code, which intercepts that message and then uses it raise a "nonzero returncode error".
* Thus the final output message of nonzero return code is less helpful to debugging runs than the original text of max errors hit.

## Error message

The stack trace I get back is:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):\n  File \"/projects/matqm/matmethods_env/codes/fireworks/fireworks/core/rocket.py\", line 224, in run\n    m_action = t.run_task(my_spec)\n  File \"/projects/matqm/matmethods_env/codes/atomate/atomate/vasp/firetasks/run_calc.py\", line 167, in run_task\n    c.run()\n  File \"/projects/matqm/matmethods_env/codes/custodian/custodian/custodian.py\", line 323, in run\n    .format(self.total_errors, ex))\nRuntimeError: 1 errors reached: (CustodianError(...), u'Job return code is 1. Terminating...'). Exited...\n
```

You can see that it's difficult to know from above that max_errors was reached and that is why we are exiting. You can figure it out though if you look at custodian.py line 323.

## Files

The run is located in :
/projects/ps-matqm/prod_runs/block_2016-12-20-23-00-16-536064/launcher_2016-12-20-23-00-35-095234/launcher_2016-12-21-09-28-04-031609

## Suggested solution (if known)

* Actually on first glance I am not even sure why this is happening. As far as I can tell, when line 323 throws an exception, the line of code about return code validation should never even run.

issue_title: Weird (incorrect?) watershed behavior
issue_body:
Hi all,

This is something I might be inclined to tackle at some point, but I currently lack the bandwidth, so I thought I'd report it here. Essentially, it looks like the scikit-image 3D watershed function is dealing improperly with plateaus, though I could not reproduce this with some "toy" plateaus. Anyway, here's an EM image of some neurites:

![screen shot 2013-10-25 at 11 09 07 pm](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/492549/1418042/536469ae-3fa7-11e3-8f48-3b740187e983.png)

And here's the (inverted) boundary probability map generated by a voxel-level classifier (using the cubehelix colormap):

![screen shot 2013-10-25 at 11 11 14 pm](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/492549/1418049/82cd84c8-3fa7-11e3-8113-84f94b92282c.png)

You can see that the boundaries are rather thick. The probability is 1.0 across the whole boundary, ie it's a plateau. Here's the output from `skimage.morphology.watershed`:

issue_title: Clean up tests
issue_body:
Let's drag the part about test cleanups from #351 to a new issue....
```
def test_content_type_with_callable_returning_scalar(self):
    # test that using a callable for content_type works as well
    # now, the callable returns a scalar instead of a list
    app = TestApp(main({}))
    response = app.put('/service6', headers={'Content-Type': 'audio/*'},
                       status=415)
    error_description = response.json['errors'][0]['description']
```

@almet said:
after seing this twice, I wonder if we should make it a helper function that we could put in cornice/tests/support.py instead. What do you think?

Proposal:
I'm thinking the same. Wouldn't it be good to define the services required for testing all the nitty-gritty details completely at runtime? When considering this, i can imagine targeting these topics as eventual goals of the test cleanup task:

- The */serviceX* jungle in ``validationapp.py`` would be completely cleaned up, the services will be defined at runtime from the particular test case in ``test_validation.py`` by using a helper function from ``cornice/tests/support.py``, as you suggested. When we're already at spawning the appropriate service at test execution time, let's also revamp the uri namespace to something like */test/service/accept* (former uris */service2* and */service3*) or */test/service/content-type* (former */service5*), to name just two examples to improve on "naming things".
- Just a **single decorator-based** service definition would remain inside the revamped ``validationapp.py`` for testing the decorator machinery against all available http verbs on a single service uri */test/service/decorated*.

I think it is sufficient to have the decorator-based service definition tested only once while testing all the other aspects of Cornice against services created at runtime. I believe this would cleanup and streamline the testing code and make its handling a bit easier.

Does this sound good to you? As we didn't define services at runtime before and were just using the decorator-style definition in our projects, some hints about doing this would definitively help. Currently, i can't get my head around **not** using Venusian for scanning the service decorator definitions. Thanks in advance!

Originally by _[Thorsten (@tsgit)](https://github.com/tsgit)_ on 2013-04-08

a search for a string with an EN DASH instead of ascii hyphen like  _Cockcroft–Walton_

leads to invalid basic search units, e.g.: `[\'+\', \'cockcroft\\xe2\', \'\', \'w\']`

``` python
basic_search_units =  '[[\'+\', \'cockcroft\\xe2\\x80\\x93w\', 
\'\', \'w\'], [\'+\', \'co\', \'\', \'w\'], [\'+\', 
\'cockcroft\\xe2\\x80\\x93walt\', \'\', \'w\'], [\'+\', \'cockc\',
```

issue_title: Allow mapping payload username field to actual
issue_body:
I have a custom User model where `USERNAME_FIELD = 'email'` however I'd like to be able to submit login credentials as:

```
{
  username: 'foo@example.com',
  password: 'mypassword'
}
```

Not

```
{
  email: 'foo@example.com',
  password: 'mypassword'
}
```

I'm wanting to make the request compliant with [OAuth2 Access Token Request](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.3.2). 

Currently this is not possible as `JSONWebTokenSerializer` creates it's username field by inspecting `UserModel.USERNAME_FIELD`. Having a setting for mapping payload username to model username would be useful.

issue_title: Enable AppVeyor CI for Windows testing
Cross-version compatibility for `subprocess.getstatusoutput`
issue_body:
When Windows support was added to `subprocess.getstatusoutput` we inadvertently made it backwards incompatible with the behaviour of the 2.7 version: https://bugs.python.org/issue22635

Now, backwards compatibility *within* the 3.x series means we can't readily change it back to make it match the Python 2.7 behaviour (even though the only reason it changed in the first place was due to a gap in our test coverage).

This impacts subprocess32 in two ways:

1. You likely want to make sure you *don't* backport that mistake (since it would break migrations from the 2.7 stdlib to `subprocess32`)
2. If `subprocess32` were to support 3.x in addition to 2.7, it could provide a way for affected applications to readily work around the problem

I'm not sure how difficult the latter would be, though.

issue_title: Upgrade to pip 8
issue_body:
I'm creating this issue just to let pip-accel users know that I'm aware of pip 8 being released and will be upgrading pip-accel to use pip 8 soon. For now everything is fine due to the way pip-accel pins the pip dependency (pip will just spam messages to the terminal about not being up to date). Judging by [pip's release notes](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/news/) most of the changes shouldn't have a big impact on pip-accel. Only the following items might prove to be relevant and/or tricky from a first cursory reading:

- Implement a top-level `pip download` command and deprecate `pip install --download`.
- Include the functionality of `peep` into pip, allowing hashes to be baked into a requirements file and ensuring that the packages being downloaded match one of those hashes. This is an additional, opt-in security measure that, when used, removes the need to trust the repository.

issue_body:
In the gtk2 version, GtkFrames (there's plenty of them in Preferences) had their title displayed only and the frame itself hidden (GTK_SHADOW_NONE).

In the gtk3 version, the frame itself is shown (probably GTK_SHADOW_ETCHED_IN) resulting in a much more crowded look.

Unless this change was intentional, I'd consider reverting to the previous look.

issue_title: Suggestion to move to semantic style versioning
issue_body:
The jira package is used extensively by other projects, and has become the defacto python module for JIRA REST communications. Unfortunately, with the release bump versioning currently in place, there are often new features, breaking changes, and new bugs introduced in 'patch' version bumps. (v1.0.11 is a recent example, which contains many new features, and breaking bugs such as #501)

This makes it hard to track regressions when using this module for critical work, and is often pinned to an exact release version for this reason. With travisci and decent code coverage in place right now, I suggest that the package move to a semantic version style so that expectations of major, minor, and patch version bumps are met.

I do not expect that older versions will necessarily receive bug-fixes, but the ability to update to bugfix/patch releases without receiving new features or incompatible changes would be desirable.

issue_title: test if (or how much) the stoichiometric matrix is ill-conditioned
issue_body:
As pointed out by Steffen Klamt in the google document:

> "I find always useful is the absolute maximum and the absolute (non-zero) minimum of coefficients in the stoichiometric matrix since they tell us something about matrix conditioning and may point to potential numerical issues."

I think this is a very relevant point. I have also often encountered problems with numerical issues that were associated with ill-conditioned matrices (most times associated with the biomass coefficients). 

The condition number can be calculated in different ways:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condition_number

For this particular purpose, I think the absolute ratio between the largest and smallest coefficients should be sufficient. 

For instance, for iJO1366 the result is 2.7e7. From my (limited) experience, something above 1e9 should raise some flags...

issue_title: Should we make Project a real thing in gcloud? Particular for gcloud.storage?
issue_body:
Our discussions in the past have said that we want `Connection` to remain stateless and really just handle sending data over the wire as well as authentication.

However, if you think about it, now that we're planning to push the "transformation" (from Python objects -> JSON) down to Buckets, Blobs, and ACLs, the connection object isn't really just doing transport, it's also representing a connection to a specific project.

Do you guys think it's worthwhile to make "project" a first-class citizen so that it makes more sense to do things like `project.get_all_buckets()` ?

This leads to a question of "what is the relationship with connections and projects? We could have a one-to-one between them (where what you really do is instantiate a project and under the hood it instantiates a connection which handles auth, etc). Or we could have a singleton connection that all the projects use with their own set of credentials...?

I am +0 on doing this kind of thing, and only just thought of it now given we need a "clean" place to put `get_all_buckets()` -- so feel free to close out as "stfu, that's dumb".

/cc @dhermes

I've used Dill happily a few times, so thank you for providing this great tool. However, the question has arisen at work; can Dill serialise and deserialise across python versions? I need to know how to pass in a BaseRecordSet object with property AliasTarget=True.

issue_title: Building with both internal and 3rd party scalac plugin not working
issue_body:
Tried with both 1.3.0.dev13 (what I'm actually using) and 1.3.0.dev16.

`pants.ini`:
```
[GLOBAL]
pants_version: 1.3.0.dev13

[compile.scalafmt]
skip: True

[compile.zinc]
scalac_plugins: [
    'wartremover',
  ]

scalac_plugin_args: {
    'wartremover': [
        'traverser:org.wartremover.warts.Unsafe',
      ]
  }

[cache]
read: False
write: False
```

`BUILD`:
```
scala_library(
    name='MyLibrary',
    sources=globs('MyClass.scala'),
    dependencies=[
        ':MyPlugin'
    ],
    scalac_plugins=[
        'myPlugin',
    ],
)

scalac_plugin(
    name='MyPlugin',
    sources=globs('MyPlugin.scala'),
    classname='foo.MyPlugin',
    plugin='myPlugin',
)

jar_library(
  name='scalac-plugin-dep',
  jars=[scala_jar('org.wartremover', 'wartremover', '2.0.3'),],
)
```

`MyPlugin.scala`:
```
package foo

import tools.nsc.plugins.{Plugin, PluginComponent}
import tools.nsc.Global

class MyPlugin(val global: Global) extends Plugin {
  val name = "myPlugin"
  val description = "Test garbage plugin"
  val components = List[PluginComponent]()
}
```

```
package bar
```

issue_title: Reporting information should survive a clean-all
issue_body: clean-all is still used by developers as a catch-all fix for pants issues. It would be useful for reporting information like the error log to survive clean-alls. Then there would be more info available to debug problems in cases where clean-all was used.

Here's an example template with some literals redacted.

```
---

        Ref: WorkerConfig
      MinSize:
        Ref: NumWorkers
      MaxSize:
        Ref: NumWorkers
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: 'Garden Worker'
          PropagateAtLaunch: 'true'
    CreationPolicy:
      ResourceSignal:
        Timeout: PT15M
        Count:
          Ref: NumWorkers

  WorkerConfig:

    Metadata:
      AWS::CloudFormation::Authentication:
        S3AccessCreds:
          type: S3
          roleName:
            Ref: WorkerInstanceRole
      AWS::CloudFormation::Init:

              cwd: "/usr/local/bin/"
              ignoreErrors: 'false'
    Properties:
      AssociatePublicIpAddress: 'true'
      KeyName: 'XXXXXX'
      ImageId: XXXXXXXX
      SecurityGroups:
        - Ref: WorkerSecurityGroup
      InstanceType:

        Ref: WorkerInstanceProfile

        - DeviceName: "/dev/xvda"

      UserData:
```

issue_title: consider making learner sign-in feature a 'fuzzy' search
issue_body:
Currently, the auto-complete of learner usernames only begins when three characters are entered, and only matches usernames that exactly start with those characters. Since the purpose is to help learners get in easily, and typos/forgotten usernames will be common, recommend implementing this as a "fuzzy" search where matches can occur anywhere in the string, begin searching after a single character, and don't need to be contiguous.

This would require a bit more optimization in the way we perform lookups, and also some smart sorting and filtering so the user doesn't see a list of hundreds of matches.

issue_title: Membership ?user and ?collection filters don't filter anything
issue_body:
When analyzing #3271, I noticed that the filters for the Membership API don't seem to filter anything out e.g.

```
/api/membership/?user=abcd123
```

returns all Memberships.

This is a problem because the `removeClassUser` action relies on this filter, so removing a single user removes all Memberships in the system.

On `learner-lessons`, I patched this particular issue with this change to `MembershipFilter`

```
class MembershipFilter(FilterSet):
    user_ids = CharFilter(method="filter_user_ids")

    def filter_user_ids(self, queryset, name, value):
        return queryset.filter(user_id__in=value.split(','))

    class Meta:
        model = Membership
        fields = ('user', 'collection', 'user_ids',)

```

issue_title: Old default value for API version in documentation
issue_body:
In documentation, there is old default value for API version (`1.24`), but [in constants.py](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/client.py#L61) there is `DEFAULT_DOCKER_API_VERSION = '1.26'` (which is actually used as a default).

[documentation latest/client](http://docker-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/client.html):
```
 from_env()
    Return a client configured from environment variables.
    The environment variables used are the same as those used by the Docker command-line client. They are:
    DOCKER_HOST
        The URL to the Docker host.
    DOCKER_TLS_VERIFY
        Verify the host against a CA certificate.
    DOCKER_CERT_PATH
        A path to a directory containing TLS certificates to use when connecting to the Docker host.
    Parameters:	
        version (str) – The version of the API to use. Set to auto to automatically detect the server’s version. Default: 1.24
```

[client.py](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/client.py#L61):
```python
 @classmethod
    def from_env(cls, **kwargs):
        """
        Return a client configured from environment variables.
        The environment variables used are the same as those used by the
        Docker command-line client. They are:
        .. envvar:: DOCKER_HOST
            The URL to the Docker host.
        .. envvar:: DOCKER_TLS_VERIFY
            Verify the host against a CA certificate.
        .. envvar:: DOCKER_CERT_PATH
            A path to a directory containing TLS certificates to use when
            connecting to the Docker host.
        Args:
            version (str): The version of the API to use. Set to ``auto`` to
                automatically detect the server's version. Default: ``1.24``
            timeout (int): Default timeout for API calls, in seconds.
            ssl_version (int): A valid `SSL version`_.
            assert_hostname (bool): Verify the hostname of the server.
            environment (dict): The environment to read environment variables
                from. Default: the value of ``os.environ``
```

issue_title: Regarding computation of concurrent_io
issue_body:
Hi,

I just found that the computation of concurrent_io is actually util_percentage in its decimal format.

In the following code,
            metrics['concurrent_io'] = (metrics['reads_per_second']
                                        + metrics['writes_per_second']
                                        ) * (metrics['service_time']
                                             / 1000.0)

where (reads_per_second + writes_per_second) = iops = io / time_delta,
service_time = io_milliseconds / io

so concurrent_io is io_milliseconds / time_delta / 1000.0, which is actually util_percentage.

issue_title: Proposal: Fine Grained Permissions
issue_body:
Right now, there are  two permission levels in deis:
- administrators
  - full access
- normal users
  - full access to create apps and manage their own apps.

This proposal would overhaul the permission system by allowing much more finer controller over which users can do.
#### Cluster Permissions
- certs
  - add or remove certs from the cluster
- apps
  - create/destroy
  - app management: view, modify, share, and transfer other user's apps.
#### App permissions
- config
  - read or modify config
- push
  - can push code or create a release
- domains
  - add or remove domains
- scale
  - scale an app up or down
#### Default Permissions

Administrators have all permissions granted.

An ETCD setting would set the default permissions for new users:

Example key layout: 

```
/deis/controller/permissions/apps true
/deis/controller/permissions/certs true
...
```
#### Example Usage

``` console
# an admin shares user cert permission with user foo
$ deis perms:create foo --cert --apps
# an app owner removes config permission from user tester
$ deis perms:delete tester --config
# users can view what permissions they have
$ deis perms:view
Cluster Wide Permissions
===================
certs

App epic-app Permissions
====================
config
push
scale

# admins and app owners can also view a users permissions
$ deis perm:list --username=foo
App epic-app Permissions
====================
config
push
scale
```
#### Testing

Almost all of this code resides in the controller, so it would mostly involve lots of tests in the controller to make sure all the edge cases are covered.
#### Migration

Migration should be pretty simple, admins would still have all access, and a migration script would grant all existing users their current permissions.

The same would apply for apps, the app owner would have all access and users who had the app shared would get the subset of permissions they already had.

issue_title: Azure: Switch provision scripts over to using ARM template
issue_body:
Currently, the Azure provision script uses v1 of the Azure API, which from what I understand is being deprecated in favor of [Azure Resource Manager](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/resource-group-overview/). We should pull in the ARM [template](https://github.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/pull/473) that's already been written for Deis 1.9, polish it, and use it in the project in place of the existing Python script.

I'd also like to use Terraform instead of the Azure CLI to submit the template, but it appears that Terraform doesn't yet support any ARM resources for Azure: https://terraform.io/docs/providers/azure/index.html

Using the ARM template will also address concerns such as #4332 (using an availability set for Deis) and #3323.

/cc @sgoings

issue_title: NaN objects in object columns of pandas dataframes from read_csv()
issue_body:
When trying to use `executemanycolumns()` with a pandas dataframe like this:

```
df = pd.read_csv(localfilepath,compression=csvfmt.compression,header=csvfmt.headerline,sep=csvdialect.delimiter,quotechar=csvdialect.quotechar,quoting=csvdialect.quoting,encoding=csvfmt.encoding,infer_datetime_format=True)

#some datetime conversion here

TURBODBC_OPTIONS = turbodbc.make_options(read_buffer_size=turbodbc.Megabytes(100),
                                                         parameter_sets_to_buffer=20000)
connection = turbodbc.connect(dsn=dsn, turbodbc_options=TURBODBC_OPTIONS)
cursor_local = connection.cursor()

to_insert = [(c, v) for c, v in df.iteritems()]
columns = [v.values for _, v in to_insert]
query = "INSERT INTO {}.{} ({}) VALUES ({})".format(schema,table,','.join([c for c, _ in to_insert]),
                                                                 ','.join(['?' for _, _ in to_insert]))
cursor.executemanycolumns(query,columns)
```

I get `RuntimeError: Unable to cast Python instance to C++ type (complie in debug mode for details)`.

The issue turned out to be that `read_csv()`represents missing strings as `NaN`objects in `object`columns rather than `None`objects.

It would be great if turbodbc could deal with those directly.

issue_title: Switching to a better peer mid-sync
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

@pipermerriam 's comment:

> As it stands I think that we may start syncing with peer A and then connect to peer B which has a higher TD. At this point we are locked into syncing with A even though B is the better peer.

(where better := higher total difficulty)

### How can it be fixed

Each time, after adding headers to the task queue (in `trinity.sync.common.chain`), check if the current peer is the highest td peer. If so, continue as before. Otherwise, break out of `_sync` and start again with the better peer.

issue_title: FEATURE: `fails()` method modifier (for testing)
issue_body:
So, I've been playing around with web3 in an attempt to setup a py.test plugin leveraging web3 and eth-tester. A fairly common scenario in my testing is to check that a method call fails when attempted. I have a pytest fixture `assert_tx_failed` that takes a snapshot, attempts the call, records whether it raises a specified exception (default is `EthereumTester.TransactionFailed`, but overridable), and reverts to the snapshot, returning whether the recorded call was successful or not. I usually use it like this:
```python
def test_foo(assert_tx_failed):
    c = contract_fixture('Foo')
    assert_tx_failed(c.foo()) # Makes a call to 'foo()' in 'Foo' contract
```
I think it would be nicer if there was a modifier in there that did this (perhaps under the umbrella of the `ImplicitContract` class I added, renamed to something like `TestContract`). It would then look like this:
```python
def test_foo():
    c = contract_fixture('Foo')
    assert c.foo().fails() # returns True only if EthereumTester.TransactionFailed occurs
    assert c.foo().fails(exception=FooException) # returns True only if that exception occurs
    # NOTE: IIRC there is only the one possible exception from Ethereum
```
Conceptually, this would be a lot more straightforward in a testing scenario, and reduces a commonly used test fixture to be built-in (reducing overall codebase management).


Side note: building a py-test plugin seems like it might be fairly easy leveraging web3 and eth-tester with some custom fixture-generation scheme leveraging the standard abi/bin compiler output (stored in JSON) to generate contract fixtures for specific contracts in your directory

issue_title: Check all dependencies of conda with a test of a working conda
issue_body:
This is from https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/issues/666#issuecomment-221294317

Another suggestion: all packages which are in the dependency chain of conda should get a test which installs conda (not sure if that's possible in a test environment) and use it to install and remove/downgrade a package. That way the real disaster of installing a broken package into the root environment which disables conda itself could be prevented.

issue_title: Cyclic dependencies not reported as a warning and things get installed in the wrong order.
issue_body:
The following exception gets raised but I guess it's not getting caught, or at least it's not getting reported ..

```
    if len(data) != 0:
        msg = 'Cyclic dependencies exist among these items: {}'
        raise CondaValueError(msg.format(' -> '.join(repr(x) for x in data.keys())))
```

This is a very important warning that identifies that the package(s) need to be fixed.

Best regards, Andrey.
