issue_title: Activate enable the wrong version of python
issue_body:
This is related to issue #166 which has been opened with the following:

> Having a symlink to conda in every environment would allow to only have that environment's bin directory added to PATH, and would avoid potential confusion.

And closed with

> Each environment has a symlink to conda, activate, and deactivate, and activate removes the root environment from the PATH.

So on a machine which has its own (old) python in /usr/bin running 

`source activate blah`

removes the root environment from PATH (as described above in the close issue #166 message) and that causes the wrong version of python being active (and a bunch of other stuff). I would suggest adding everything else from the root environment to the environment, but of course not removing the root environment may be a sensible choice too

issue_title: Export Pcap functions needed to capture specifying an internal buffer size
issue_body:
The goal of this task is to export more Pcap functions (so they are available from Python) in order to open a live capture in the same way than calling pcap_open_live function but being able to specify the internal buffer size. In a way, pcap_open_live is a wrap-up for many of these functions but it's not as flexible as manually calling each one.

The functions we have listed to achieve this goal are the following:

- [pcap_create](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_create)
- [pcap_set_snaplen](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_set_snaplen)
- [pcap_set_promisc](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_set_promisc)
- [pcap_set_timeout](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_set_timeout)
- [pcap_set_buffer_size](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_set_buffer_size)
- [pcap_activate](https://linux.die.net/man/3/pcap_activate)

Please check that this functions are both available from Linux Pcap and WinPcap. If not, add pre-processor directives to avoid compilation or linking issues.

issue_title: DeleteSnapshot API timing out, failing immediately on retry
issue_body:
I'm trying to delete snapshots on a recurring schedule via the Python API on AWS Lambda. I'm hitting long wait times for the deletion to complete. It eventually will fail with a 504 gateway timeout but then hits a 404 on retry because the snapshot no longer exists.

Example sequence for a single snapshot:
```
2017-04-25T00:59:51.214Z Deleting snapshot logs-20170424221413...
2017-04-25T01:00:51.85Z DELETE http://mysescluster:9200/_snapshot/some_snapshots/logs-20170424221413 [status:504 request:59.871s]
2017-04-25T01:00:51.258Z DELETE http://myescluster:9200/_snapshot/some_snapshots/logs-20170424221413 [status:404 request:0.173s]
```

The 1 minute timeout is coming from my Elasticsearch cluster being behind an AWS ELB with a 1 minute idle timeout. I can bump that and the Elasticsearch connection timeout to give it more room.

What's confusing though is that the Snapshot seems to delete since the retry hits a 404. So it looks like the delete succeeds but doesn't finish the request. 

Is there any way to avoid waiting for completion when calling `DeleteSnapshot` via API? It doesn't look like this method supports it in the underlying ES transport method.

Elasticsearch version: 5.2.2
Curator version: 5.0.1
Elasticsearch-py version: 5.1.0

issue_title: empty list in add or remove filter causes alias action to fail
issue_body:
Already discussed this internally with @untergeek , opening it up for a public discussion.

Creating an action to add and remove indices to an alias (e.g. last 7 days), is an atomic operation and fails if one of the filers receives an empty list. I.e. if this action is run, while the cluster only has indices for the last 5 days, the `remove` filter would fail, causing the whole action to fail.  

Also reported via: https://discuss.elastic.co/t/curator-alias-add-not-working-if-remove-list-is-empty/73549

Example config:
```
actions:
        last-7-days: 
                action: alias 
                description: "Add / remove indexes from last-7-days alias"
                options: 
                        name: last-7-days 
                        ignore_empty_list: True 
                add: 
                        filters: 
                                - filtertype: pattern 
                                  kind: prefix 
                                  value: logstash- 
                                - filtertype: age 
                                  direction: younger 
                                  source: name 
                                  timestring: '%Y.%m.%d' 
                                  unit_count: 7 
                                  unit: days 
                remove: 
                        filters: 
                                - filtertype: pattern 
                                  kind: prefix 
                                  value: losgstash- 
                                - filtertype: age 
                                  direction: older 
                                  source: name 
                                  timestring: '%Y.%m.%d' 
                                  unit_count: 7 
                                  unit: days
``` 

On one side I feel this behavior is correct, the action instructs curator to do these two steps and since one is failing, the whole actions fails. Just continuing with one filter failed, could lead to unexpected behavior. E.g. if filters or the whole action depend on each other. 
But in case of the alias I can understand that user exception is to just add the indices and skip the remove part until there are indices in the cluster that can be removed from the index. 

Just continuing with a failed filter doesn't sound like a good approach either, even in case of aliases.
Imagine relaying on a query that uses a last 7 days alias, if the remove "silently" fails, it could lead to this alias returning results for the last 3 weeks instead, resulting in wrong data.

Purposing to make this a configurable option for the user (to be "ok" with failing a specific or any part of the action).

issue_title: Transition matrix stabilization results in un-normalized transition matrices
issue_body:
In base.py, the transition matrix is calculated from the sufficient statistics and then normalized. However, in the follow line, the transition matrix update is done element-by-element, ignoring cells which are less than eps. The result is an un-normalized transition matrix if fit() stops after this update, which breaks, e.g., score().

I just eliminated the eps - check, which means that some cells of the transition matrix will effectively go to zero. I'm not sure what the "right" thing to do is.

````
        if 't' in params:
            transmat_ = self.transmat_prior - 1.0 + stats['trans']
            normalize(transmat_, axis=1)
            self.transmat_ = np.where(self.transmat_ <= np.finfo(float).eps,
                                      self.transmat_, transmat_)
````

issue_title: HTTPS test_client doesn't run app with SSL context
issue_body:
Instantiating a test client like this: `client = await test_client(app, scheme='https')` currently doesn't work as expected. It seems that the server isn't ran with an SSL context.

## Expected behaviour
test_client should run app with an auto-generated SSL context.

## Actual behaviour
It doesn't :-)
## Steps to reproduce

```
from aiohttp import web

async def hello(request):
    return web.Response(text='Hello, world')

async def test_hello(test_client, loop):
    app = web.Application()
    app.router.add_get('/', hello)
    client = await test_client(app, scheme='https')
    resp = await client.get('/')
    assert resp.status == 200
    text = await resp.text()
    assert 'Hello, world' in text
```

issue_title: aiohttp.client does not support HTTP redirect 308 (RFC 7538)
issue_body:
Currently, `aiohttp.client` does not support recent HTTP redirect code 308 Permanent Redirect, defined in [RFC 7538](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7538). It is simply a permanent version of already handled 307 redirect, or a legacy-free version of 301 redirect.

I would also argue that `normalize_path_middleware` default redirect class should be changed to `HTTPPermanentRedirect`, as it is currently very surprising that a user agent will likely retry a POST request as a GET one after receiving a 301 redirect from path normalization, but that is probably a breaking API change.
Still, supporting 308 in `aiohttp.client` would allow `normalize_path_middleware` to be used with POST requests in asyncio <-> asyncio communication by specifying redirect 308 manually.

issue_title: Need possibility to modify client.Session default headers in constructed session
issue_body:
## Long story short

`client.Session._default_headers` is "protected" field, while sometimes required to modify it in runtime (set specific 'User-Agent' and etc) without restart.

## Expected behaviour

Getter property `client.Session.default_headers` (it's mutable -> setter is not needed) is suitable solution for changing headers between requests.

## Actual behaviour

`client.Session._default_headers` is used.

issue_title: Big chunk decoding makes content.iter_any() iterate over partial content
issue_body:
## Long story short

When receiving a very big chunk (response has `Transfer-Encoding: chunked.` header), result of `aiohttp.ClientSession().request(...).content.iter_any()` yields partial chunks.

## Expected behaviour

I would expect chunks integrity to be respected, and `iter_any()` to yield reasonably-decodable json content.

## Actual behaviour

`iter_any()` yields partial chunks, which is Bad(tm).

## Steps to reproduce

I think this basic code below is enough if you find a server that answers big chunks. We internally managed to reproduce the bug by querying a docker server with URL `GET /v1.24/containers/json?all=1 HTTP/1.1.`. Server would reply a very big 350 000 bytes long chunk, and instead of yielding that chunk iter_any() just served us an about-30k-bytes-long beginning of chunk.

```python
async for chunk in aiohttp.ClientSession().request(...).content.iter_any():
    json.loads(chunk)
```

If ValueError is raised, you lose the game.

## Your environment
Ubuntu Xenial (python 3.5.2) with aiohttp 2.0.7 (bug is reproducible on aiohttp master branch).

issue_title: Identify notebook file being run
issue_body:
Hi,

I've seen this type of question a lot:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/20050927/how-to-get-the-ipython-notebook-title-associated-with-the-currently-running-ipyt?rq=1

It makes sense to me that the kernel should not know what it's talking to from a design perspective.

However, I'm currently in the process of working through a Jupyter High Availability scenario. Our goal is to have two Jupyter instances running in two different VMs and switch them if one of those two VMs go down for some reason without losing the kernel state.

We have control over the kernels we are running (see https://github.com/jupyter-incubator/sparkmagic/blob/master/remotespark/wrapperkernel/sparkkernelbase.py), and we'd like to be able to tie some state (a session number) to a particular kernel instance.

It seems to me like I'd need some things to achieve this, but maybe you have better ideas:
* Fire some piece of code automatically every time a notebook starts: this could be the `__init__` method in my kernel or some other piece of code that is triggered every time a kernel gets started (some Javascript code in the notebook maybe? I know this wouldn't apply for other clients but it's a start).
* This previous bit of code that gets fired would need to always be run with the same ID to be able to identify the state it needs to reconstruct (i.e. it would need to know that for this particular kernel we had X particular state).
* Some persistent storage that both Jupyter instances could have access to.

I thought of a concrete implementation and I'd like to hear some feedback on if possible:
There is a Notebook extension that reads some ID in the notebook's page DOM (I need help knowing what ID this would be: e.g. notebook name with relative paths from root folder included or a GUID in some hidden cell in the notebook file), which would then issue a request to the kernel with this ID to restore its state. The kernel would then take this ID and get the session ID from cloud storage. If the ID is embedded in Javascript, both Jupyter servers would need to trust the notebook from the get go.

Thanks for any help or pointers you may have!
(cc. @msftristew, @MohamedElKamhawy, @ellisonbg)

issue_title: Add discovery of user's core node to authentication process
issue_body:
During the authentication process, an app verifies that the authentication response is signed by the keys authorized by the owner of the name the user is claiming. It does this by doing a lookup against a core node that the app trusts.

The authentication response also includes a core session token that should be used for requests against the core node that issues it. 

Do we have a mechanism for the app to discover the user's code?

Related: https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-browser/issues/290

tagging @jcnelson @kantai

issue_title: STACKs: implement pay-with-STACKs rules for names
issue_body:
Introduce a new namespace type (version 3) where names are purchased with STACKs tokens, instead of BTC.  The following new rules apply for names created in version-3 namespaces:

Registration:
* The `NAME_PREORDER` transaction sends a dust amount to the default burn address, instead of the name's cost on the namespace price curve.  It is otherwise unchanged from before.
* The `NAME_REGISTRATION` transaction is only accepted if the address that sent the `NAME_PREORDER` has enough STACKs to pay for it.  Otherwise, it is rejected.
* ~~When a `NAME_REGISTRATION` transaction is committed, the `NAME_PREORDER`'s address is debited the amount of STACKs the name cost.~~
* The `NAME_PREORDER` wire format will be changed to include the number of STACKs to burn in the `OP_RETURN`.
* When a `NAME_PREORDER` transaction is committed, the `NAME_PREORDER`'s address is debited the amount of STACKs it indicated to burn.

Renewals:
* A `NAME_RENEWAL` transaction sends a dust amount to the default burn address, instead of the name's cost on the namespace price curve.  It is otherwise unchanged from before.
* A `NAME_RENEWAL` transaction is only accepted if the *owner* address has enough STACKs in its account to pay for the name's price.
* When a `NAME_RENEWAL` transaction is committed, the *current owner*'s address is debited the amount of STACKs the name cost.

issue_title: Flush unitdata after every handler?
issue_body:
I've noticed during developing that if any handler fails, none of the unitdata gets flushed.  This means that on subsequent runs, states are reset to what they were at the start of the hook and even handlers that completed successfully are re-run.

I think we should flush after every successful handler to ensure that successful handlers are not re-run.  (Also note that everything is flushed before external handlers are run to ensure that they see the proper state.)

issue_title: Other developers' keys are not present on cluster after stopping and starting a cluster.
Exemple to Mesa: Animate the out put
issue_body:
After basic tutorial you had : 
for model.py :
`
# model.py
from mesa.space import MultiGrid
from mesa import Agent, Model
from mesa.time import RandomActivation
import random

class MoneyModel(Model):
    """A model with some number of agents."""
    def __init__(self, N, width, height):
        self.num_agents = N
        self.grid = MultiGrid(width, height, True)
        self.schedule = RandomActivation(self)
        # Create agents
        for i in range(self.num_agents):
            a = MoneyAgent(i, self)
            self.schedule.add(a)
            # Add the agent to a random grid cell
            x = random.randrange(self.grid.width)
            y = random.randrange(self.grid.height)
            self.grid.place_agent(a, (x, y))

    def step(self):
        self.schedule.step()

class MoneyAgent(Agent):
    """ An agent with fixed initial wealth."""
    def __init__(self, unique_id, model):
        super().__init__(unique_id, model)
        self.wealth = 1

    def move(self):
        possible_steps = self.model.grid.get_neighborhood(
            self.pos,
            moore=True,
            include_center=False)
        new_position = random.choice(possible_steps)
        self.model.grid.move_agent(self, new_position)

    def give_money(self):
        cellmates = self.model.grid.get_cell_list_contents([self.pos])
        if len(cellmates) > 1:
            other = random.choice(cellmates)
            other.wealth += 1
            self.wealth -= 1

    def step(self):
        self.move()
        if self.wealth > 0:
            self.give_money()
`
And for run.py
`# run.py
from model import MoneyModel
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


model = MoneyModel(50, 10, 10)
for i in range(20):
    model.step()

agent_counts = np.zeros((model.grid.width, model.grid.height))
for cell in model.grid.coord_iter():
    cell_content, x, y = cell
    agent_count = len(cell_content)
    agent_counts[x][y] = agent_count
plt.imshow(agent_counts, interpolation='nearest')
plt.colorbar()

# If running from a text editor or IDE, remember you'll need the following:
plt.show()
`
Now we will made this new file : 
`
# animator.py
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.animation as animation


def animator(data):
	fig = plt.figure()
	ims = []
	for add in data:
		ims.append((plt.pcolor(add),))
	im_ani = animation.ArtistAnimation(fig, ims, interval=100, repeat_delay=300, blit=True)
        # If you want save the animation
	# im_ani.save('im.mp4')
	plt.show()
`
--------=========--------

Now we need to change the run.py file to : 
`# run.py
from model import MoneyModel
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from animator import animator

frame = []

model = MoneyModel(50, 10, 10)
for i in range(20):
	model.step()
	agent_counts = np.zeros((model.grid.width, model.grid.height))
	for cell in model.grid.coord_iter():
		cell_content, x, y = cell
		agent_count = len(cell_content)
		agent_counts[x][y] = agent_count
	frame.append(agent_counts)


animator(frame)
`
Now you had a easy way to see a video, from each step of your model.

````
python -c 'import gssapi as g;g.Name("foo@bar.baz",g.NameType.kerberos_principal).display_as(g.NameType.kerberos_principal)'
````

````
    _utils.get_encoding())

````

issue_title: .Net Core 1.1.2 LTS
issue_body:
# Understanding Support for LTS

I am a little bit confused to understand the end of support date for .Net Core 1.0.5. A few days back 1.1.2 was Current before the release of 2.0. Now, this has become LTS, I believe this will change the end of support date for 1.0.5 which was LTS before. 

Does this release mean end of support for 1.0.5 will be 12 months from November 16, 2016 which is what is mentioned in the https://www.microsoft.com/net/core/support or from 14th Aug 2016.

I feel it's bit unfair to consider Nov 16, 2016, as the release date for 1.1.2 LTS as this is probably 14-Aug 2017. 

Could you please clear my doubts regarding the end of support date for .Net Core 1.0.5.

issue_title: Several code paths (conf files, interactive domains, --csr) do not set webroot_map entries correctly
issue_body:
This may or may not be intended.

If you set `webroot-path` in your config and don't specify your domains, letsencrypt fails with the following error:
```
The webroot plugin is not working; there may be problems with your existing configuration.
The error was: PluginError('--webroot-path must be set',)
```
This is pretty confusing, both because `webroot-path` is set, and also because the code in question actually uses `webroot-map`, which is autogenerated in cli.py if both webroot and domains are specified upon invocation.

Previously this use pattern worked.

These changes were introduced in e1f0fcc and ffe6226e.

issue_title: "Error creating new authz :: Syntax error" with Unicode/punycode subdomain
issue_body:
Hi,

I tried to request a cert for a punycode subdomain for 😸.op-co.de (`xn--z38h.op-co.de` as DNS-ID SAN and CN in the CSR) today, and the resulting response was:

> Error: unauthorized :: The client lacks sufficient authorization :: Error creating new authz :: Syntax error

While that host is not whitelisted, there should be a more appropriate response.

CSR (shortened):
```
# openssl req -noout -text -inform der < catface.op-co.de-2015.csr
Certificate Request:
    Data:
        Version: 0 (0x0)
        Subject: C=DE, CN=xn--z38h.op-co.de
        Subject Public Key Info:
            Public Key Algorithm: rsaEncryption
                Public-Key: (4096 bit)
                Modulus:
                    [...]
                Exponent: 65537 (0x10001)
        Attributes:
        Requested Extensions:
            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
                DNS:xn--z38h.op-co.de
    Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
         [...]
```

I'm skipping the log as @jcjones made me aware on IRC that punycode is currently [forbidden in the code](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=punycode&type=Code).

This ticket is therefore mainly for documentation purposes and to track the implementation status, should it be subject to change.

issue_title: Security housekeeping cron job
issue_body: We want to have a script that runs once per hour to perform tasks on the system that need to happen periodically, such as adjusting HSTS and HPKP intervals, and perhaps polling OCSP to trigger renewals on the basis of CA-initiated revocations.

issue_body:
Currently there is no documentation for `gettz`, which is the primary interface through which one gets Olson database zones.

Some prior art that should be cobbled together to make real documentation:

- [Here are some slides about gettz](https://pganssle.github.io/pygotham-2016-dateutil-talk/#/4/3) - [video here](https://youtu.be/X_bCto95Imc?t=20m25s) (audio is messed up)
- [Here is the actual implementation](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L1431)

Unfortunately not a whole lot to go on.

issue_title: Parsed time zone wrong when adjusted at runtime
issue_body:
Because `tz.tzlocal` works out its offset at import time, there are problems when the time zone is adjusted at runtime.

We are running a test case for a time parsing function based on `dateutil` that parametrizes the local time zone over several values, e.g. UTC, CET.

When running the UTC test, with the TZ environment variable set to UTC and `tzset()` called, a time string indicating UTC (actually 'Z') is parsed. In this instance, the returned object has tzinfo set to `tzlocal()` because the parsed timezone ('UTC') matches the local timezone.

However, this is only true for the runtime local timezone. It wasn't UTC at import time but CET, which results in the test failing.

Example:

```
$ cat /tmp/testcase.py 
#!/usr/bin/python3
from dateutil import parser
import os
from time import tzset
os.environ['TZ'] = 'UTC'
tzset()
dt = parser.parse('2015-08-24T10:41:00Z')
assert dt.timestamp() == 1440412860.0
$ python3 /tmp/testcase.py
$ TZ=CET python3 /tmp/testcase.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/testcase.py", line 8, in <module>
    assert dt.timestamp() == 1440412860.0
AssertionError
```

issue_title: Fuzzy parsing fails when string contains 'a' without hour.
issue_body:
Migrating launchpad issue [#1428895](https://bugs.launchpad.net/dateutil/+bug/1428895):

Quoting [Minqi Jiang](https://launchpad.net/~mnqjng) on 3/5/2015:
>Fuzzy parsing fails when string contains 'a' without hour, as in

    'A monkey on 10/08/1990'
    'I have a meeting on March 1'

>These sentences will raise a ValueError when parsed using the method

    dateutil.parser.parse('your string here', fuzzy=True)

>because the private _parse method in parse.py raises a ValueError if 'a' is detected (for AM) without a corresponding hour. This is not an issue in python-dateutil versions < 2.4.

issue_title: ContainsOnly should allow empty iterable
issue_body:
`ContainsOnly` raises error if an empty iterable is passed in. But an empty list does _not_ contain _other_ elements than what's in `choices`, so it should be valid.

If user wants also an empty iterable to be invalid, that can be achieved by another validator (`Length` maybe).

At least this could be made configurable, for example via `raise_on_empty` option, that would be set `True` by default (for backwards-compatibility).

issue_title: Optimization with quantified solving
issue_body:
The standard optimization problem:  min  f(x) s.t.   P(x)   (where P(x) is a quantifier-free formula)
can be encoded as the logic formula: P(x) \land \forall y . ( P(y) \to f(x) \leq f(y) )

For example, for bit-vector optimization problem, "ufbv" tactic can be applied to solve the constructed quantified formula. 

It seems the `opt` engine can provide an option for optimization with quantified SMT solving.

issue_title: Combining selecting and storing for arrays with pdr engine
issue_body:
Hi, 
I have been observing strange behaviour in z3 when combining select and store commands for arrays using the pdr fixed point engine. 

The following example is satisfiable although this should not be the case: 

```
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine pdr)

(declare-rel P ((Array Int Int)))
(declare-rel Q ((Array Int Int)))
(declare-rel R ())

(declare-var a (Array Int Int))
(declare-var x Int)

(rule (=> (= (select a 0) 1) (Q a)))
(rule (=> (and (Q a) (=  x (select a 0)) ) (P (store a 0 (+ x 1)))))
(rule( => (and (P a) (= (select a 0) 42)) R))

(query R 
       :print-certificate true
)
```

Arrays satisfying the predicate Q should have value '1' at position 0 (by rule 1)
By rule 2, arrays satisfying P should have value '2' at position 0. 
Still it holds that an array satisfying P can have value '42' at position 0. 

It seems that when using the select in the premise to store the value in a variable, all constraints on this variable are dropped when it is used as part of a store expression in the conclusion.  

In contrast, a direct nesting as in the following example seems to work: 

```
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine pdr)


(declare-rel P ((Array Int Int)))
(declare-rel Q ((Array Int Int)))
(declare-rel R ())

(declare-var a (Array Int Int))
(declare-var x Int)

(rule (=> (= (select a 0) 1) (Q a)))
(rule (=> (Q a) (P (store a 0 (+ (select a 0) 1)))))
(rule( => (and (P a) (= (select a 0) 42)) R))

(query R 
       :print-certificate true
)
```

```
(declare-const x Float32)
(assert (= x (fp #b1 #b00000010 #b01100001000011000001011)))
;; This is a small normal number
;; Rational(-11568651, 356811923176489970264571492362373784095686656)

(declare-const y Float32)
(assert (= y (fp #b1 #b00000000 #b10110100101110000011101)))
;; This is a small subnormal number
;; Rational(-5921821, 713623846352979940529142984724747568191373312)

;; r = (f.add RNA x y)
;;
;;          nextDown(r)        nextUp(r)
;;         /         r        /
;;        /         /        /        x        y     -0 +0
;; <------|----|-|--|--------|-- .. --|-- .. --|-- .. |--|------>
;;             / \
;;            /   \
;;   halfpoint     (+ (to_real x) (to_real y))
;;
;; Given the above picture, I believe r should be r, and definitely
;; not nextUp(r).

(declare-const result Float32)
(assert (= result (fp.add RNA x y)))
(assert (not (= result (fp #b1 #b00000010 #b10111011011010000011010))))
(check-sat)
(exit)
```

In particular, Z3 (4d07fa5db3eb52bcadb0ab618699d2de3fb4addb) thinks that the result is actually nextUp(result). I know RNA results are awkward to check, so I am also definitely not ruling out a mistake on my end :)

issue_title: Allow compound data type specification in DatasetSpec
issue_body:
To allow table datasets, the *DatasetSpec* class will need to be modified to allow specification of compound types.

The argument *dtype* should be modified to allow a list of DataTypeSpec objects. A DataTypeSpec should hold the data type, label, and documentation about the column.

The following is an example usage of specifying a compound data type

```python
nwb_ds = DatasetSpec(doc='dataset_doc',
                     dtype=[DataTypeSpec(dtype=int, label=neuron_id, doc='a column for neuron ID'),
                            DataTypeSpec(dtype=float, label='voltage', doc='a column for IC voltage')],
                     name='data')
```

**Note:** *NWBDatasetSpec* will also need to be updated to allow these options to be passed through

issue_title: Updating the currency of an existing object fails
issue_body:
We are trying to update the currency of an existing object (django-money 0.7.6), this fails because of a specific check in the set method, what is the idea of this check?

    def __set__(self, obj, value):
        if isinstance(value, tuple):
            value = Money(amount=value[0], currency=value[1])
        if isinstance(value, Money):
            obj.__dict__[self.field.name] = value.amount
            # we have to determine whether to replace the currency.
            # i.e. if we do the following:
            # .objects.get_or_create(money_currency='EUR')
            # then the currency is already set up, before this code hits
            # __set__ of MoneyField. This is because the currency field
            # has less creation counter than money field.
            obj_curr = obj.__dict__[self.currency_field_name]
            val_curr = str(value.currency)
            def_curr = str(self.field.default_currency)
            if obj_curr != val_curr:
                # in other words, update the currency only if it wasn't
                # changed before.
                if obj_curr == def_curr:   # <=== why ????
                setattr(
                    obj, self.currency_field_name,
                    smart_unicode(value.currency))

issue_title: Installing initialization packages
issue_body:
Hi all,

Very much appreciate your effort here!

In the article [Using Hail on the Google Cloud Platform](http://discuss.hail.is/t/using-hail-on-the-google-cloud-platform/80), you suggest that I can install additional initialization packages by writing a script similar to your own gs://hail-common/hail-init.sh and providing it in a comma-separated list. Now that I'm using cloudtools, I similarly offer my script with yours via the --init option.

I've tried doing that where I'd very much like to install the python package "google-cloud-storage" so that I can interact with files in buckets through that API. However, I've had no luck with this method; my scripts cannot import google-cloud-storage.

However, I stumbled upon a strategy that does work. If I start the cluster, connect via a notebook, and open a Hail notebook, I can run pip to install the package. After that, when I run cluster submit to send full scripts, they acknowledge presence of the package and run well.

Any idea what's going on here, and whether I can cut out the notebook step to initialize with google-cloud storage and other future packages I might like to install? Thanks!

Best,
Dave

issue_title: Managing cliquet extra requirements
issue_body:
*Cliquet* defines some extra requirements:

* ``postgresql``, for the backends
* ``monitoring`` for monitoring tooling (statsd, newrelic)

Currently, we don't pull them in *Kinto*, because we want it to be straightforward to run (i.e. ``git clone``, ``make serve``). That's why we also switched to the memory backend by default.

But, as a consequence, if someone wants to use *Kinto* with postgresql, she has to explicit pull cliquet requirements using ``pip install "cliquet[postgresql]"`` (see docs).

Ideally, we would like to add a some extra requirements to *Kinto* to ease that (for example ``pip install kinto[production]``).

Our attempts did not lead us anywhere so far. We need help!

See:
* https://github.com/mozilla-services/kinto/commit/695dde80d8b03f95b268d1c4eee46ff7f696f838 : see ``[:-1]``
* https://github.com/mozilla-services/kinto/commit/a17da2a0959638bb80c4ade46e08386b6b59f864 : repeats cliquet underlying deps (not ideal)

issue_title: trailing_slash_redirect prevent from creating views with a trailing slash in plugins.
issue_body:
Serving assets with pyramid default the subpath to ``index.html`` in case a trailing slash is present.

With ``trailing_slash_redirect`` the redirection happens before reaching the static route defined in the plugin.
We should change the trailing_slash_redirect to catch a 404 and redirect if there is a trailing slash only in that case rather than defining a catchall route that redirects any url even existing ones.

Refs: https://github.com/Kinto/kinto-dist/pull/3

issue_title: Add close_decompressor() to BufferedReader
issue_body:
The change introduces to fix #34 adds a close ArchiveIterator to, and disabled the close on underlying stream in BufferedReader. This was incorrect, instead BufferedReader.close() should continue to close the underlying stream, ~~but adding an option to not close the stream, `close_stream=False` to BufferedReader constructor.~~  ArchiveIterator will not close the underlying stream, as before.

Simpler fix: Just adding separate `close_decompressor()` method to BufferedReader, ArchiveIterator calls `close_decompressor()`, not close(). close() continues to behave as before, closing the stream.

issue_title: Tensorboard ignores misspelled flags
issue_body:
Tensorboard silently ignores misspelled flags. Whatever flag you pass to tensorboard it swallows it without even blinking. This might seem like a small problem, but it causes some real-life problems.

A real-life example - this caused significant cost increase in our case for our tensorboard installation in GCP. We run it in kubernetes cluster and we used the https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-tensorboard project as a base for our installation. Unfortunately some time recently the name of --reload-frequency parameter has changed to --reload-interval (which is BTW. much better name for what it was) but the appengine-tensorboard project have still the old parameter in configuration (I created pull request https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-tensorboard/pull/1 to fix it).

Due to silent swallowing of misspelled parameters, we have not even noticed that the parameter name has changed since the appengine-tensorboard project was released (actually there was even weird inconsistency in the appengine-tensorboard project that in one place RELOAD_INTERVAL variable was mentioned but not used). 

All-in-all - it would have been much better if tensorboard - following good practice of about few hundred thousands of other projects ;) - would fail if you specify wrong parameter.

issue_title: spinel: documentation issue with local routes property
issue_body:
PROP_THREAD_LOCAL_ROUTES (route add/remove) looks to have the same arguments as PROP_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS (prefix add/remove), but the stable parameter should probably be               prf instead: Default Router Preference, which may be: 'high', 'med', or 'low'.

Also, for PROP_THREAD_ON_MESH_NETS, the stable flag should probably just be part of the general flags parameter and follow the Thread specification definition, otherwise the API has a  conflicting redundant way of setting it.

```
D.4.12. PROP 91: PROP_THREAD_LOCAL_ROUTES

Type: Read-Write
Packed-Encoding: A(T(6CbC))
Data per item is:

6: IPv6 Prefix
C: Prefix length, in bits
b: Stable flag
C: Other flags
```

issue_title: Contribution.__init__ argument to suppress import of vocabularies and methods
issue_body: @moonshoes87 would the creation of a boolean argument to the __init__ of the Contribution object which suppresses the import of vocabularies and methods be possible? I don't know if there is hard coded portions of the Contribution object which would shatter into a million pieces if this happened or what, but this change would allow for giant increases in runtime for the GUIs that don't need these vocabularies and simply use the Contribution object to read and write tables. It would also help with the magnetometer file conversion to magic files as I wouldn't need to import these things when writing to disk. I could work around this in the case of conversion scripts by using just the MagicDataFrame object, but that wouldn't fix the delay this feature has in the GUIs. Obviously this is a low priority objective, but right now the runtime of the GUIs in 3.0 is significantly slower than in 2.5 even running tests takes 2-3 times longer just because starting is so much slower. issue_title: Update cpu and memory requests after cluster creation
issue_body:
On our Pangeo deployment users get a default `worker-template.yaml` which allows them to create clusters by simply running `cluster = KubeCluster()` without having to worry about what a kubernetes even is.

However in some occasions people want to be able to update the memory and cpu ratios of their workers depending on what they are running. The current workflow for this is to either specify the whole template as a dict or to copy the default `worker-template.yaml`, understand it, update the values and then user `KubeCluster.from_yaml()`.

Personally I ended up writing a couple of helper functions in my notebook which look like this:

```python
def update_worker_memory(cluster, new_limit):
    cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].resources.limits["memory"] = new_limit
    cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].resources.requests["memory"] = new_limit
    if '--memory-limit' in cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args:
        index = cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args.index('--memory-limit')
        cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args[index + 1] = new_limit
    return cluster

def update_worker_cpu(cluster, new_limit):
    cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].resources.limits["cpu"] = new_limit
    cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].resources.requests["cpu"] = new_limit
    if '--nthreads' in cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args:
        index = cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args.index('--nthreads')
        cluster.pod_template.spec.containers[0].args[index + 1] = new_limit
    return cluster
```

This allows me to adjust the worker template after the cluster has been created and all new workers will follow the updated values.

I'm considering how to add this functionality into the core project. I'm inspired by the [dask-jobqueue SLURMCluster](http://dask-jobqueue.readthedocs.io/en/latest/generated/dask_jobqueue.SLURMCluster.html#dask_jobqueue.SLURMCluster) which allows you to specify cores and memory as kwargs. Therefore perhaps @mrocklin, @jhamman or @guillaumeeb have thoughts.

Before I go charging in to raise a PR I would like to discuss options.

- Would it be useful to add methods to the `KubeCluster` object to update sizes after creation as I am above?
- Should we add kwargs to the cluster init and if so should they create the cluster and use the helpers or update the config before creation?
- Are there any other ways of specifying memory and cpu that I haven't captured in the examples above?

issue_body:
Hi,

I'd like to suggest the addition of a directory for storing hook scripts executed after a certificate renewal (for example `/etc/letsencrypt/hooks.d`). This would allow third party packages to easily integrate with certbot without overloading the `--post-hook` parameter in the cron file. For example a tomcat package could install a script in this directory to create a PKCS12 keystore in `/etc/tomcat` from the pem files and restart the Tomcat connector after each renewal.

issue_title: Proper python file encoding definition
issue_body:
All raiden python files seem to be using the [PEP 263](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0263/) encoding definition for UTF-8.

It's a minor request but the encoding is misspelt in all of the files. `utf8` is not the correct name. The encoding must always contain a dash, unless it's used in systems where dash is not acceptable. The correct name is `utf-8`. (source: [IANA](http://www.iana.org/assignments/character-sets/character-sets.xhtml))

So all 

```python
# -*- coding: utf8 -*-
```

should be replaced with

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
```

And since we are at it I would also suggest adding the python shebang on top there.
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
```


This is not just to be pedantic. Some editors (e.g.: emacs), don't recognize the requested encoding and need specific tweaks to work with the raiden sourcecode at the moment.

issue_title: custom_head and precompute=True: Issue saving precomputed weights
issue_body:
I had some trouble using ConvLearner.pretrained() with 'precompute=True' and a 'custom_head'. After the first predicted batch finished computing bcolz complained with: 
```python 
'fastai\model.py in predict_to_bcolz(m, gen, arr, workers)' @ arr.append(y)
     ValueError: array trailing dimensions do not match with self.
```

I think I found the following lines to be the root cause:
```python
class ConvnetBuilder():
    def __init__(...):
[...]
        self.nf = model_features[f] if f in model_features else (num_features(layers)*2)
        if not custom_head: layers += [AdaptiveConcatPool2d(), Flatten()]
[...]
```
Without the flatten layer precompute doesn't seem to work. Additionally it looks like the previous line expects the AdaptiveConcatPool2d to be part of the top_model, since it doubles the number of expectd features from the last 'top_model' layer.

I couldn't find an easy workaround without creating a derived class which doesn't call the base class initializer.

issue_title: metrics for mixedinput classification
issue_body:
Someone raised the issue [here](http://forums.fast.ai/t/structured-learner-for-kaggle-titanic/8378/37) that we don't have metrics functions for mixedinput classification (and we don't have any notebooks demonstrating this class of DL). 

It appears that same as `accuracy/accuracy_np` - we need multidimensional torch preds support (plus these come in as log_preds). I implemented a version that works, but need help with better naming, before submitting PR.

```
def recall_torch(preds, targs, thresh=0.5):
    pred_pos = torch.max(preds > thresh, dim=1)[1]
    tpos = torch.mul((targs.byte() == pred_pos.byte()), targs.byte())
    return tpos.sum()/targs.sum()

def precision_torch(preds, targs, thresh=0.5):
    pred_pos = torch.max(preds > thresh, dim=1)[1]
    tpos = torch.mul((targs.byte() == pred_pos.byte()), targs.byte())
    return tpos.sum()/pred_pos.sum()

def log_fbeta_torch(log_preds, targs, beta, thresh=0.5):
    assert beta > 0, 'beta needs to be greater than 0'
    beta2 = beta ** 2
    preds = torch.exp(log_preds)
    rec = recall_torch(preds, targs, thresh)
    prec = precision_torch(preds, targs, thresh)
    return (1 + beta2) * prec * rec / (beta2 * prec + rec)

def log_f1_torch(log_preds, targs, thresh=0.5): return log_fbeta_torch(log_preds, targs, 1, thresh)
```
usage:

```
m.fit(lr, 2, cycle_len=2, cycle_mult=3, metrics=[accuracy,log_f1_torch])
100% 8/8 [00:00<00:00, 12.92it/s]
epoch      trn_loss   val_loss   accuracy   log_f1_torch 
    0      0.472473   0.438977   0.780899   0.722966  
    1      0.429974   0.426494   0.814607   0.763082  
    2      0.417189   0.444517   0.820225   0.778917  
    3      0.372991   0.430631   0.825843   0.7864    
    4      0.323502   0.440252   0.831461   0.791569  
    5      0.284224   0.49835    0.837079   0.806387  
    6      0.251587   0.454803   0.814607   0.760272  
    7      0.23454    0.459216   0.814607   0.760272  

Out[44]:
[array([0.45922]), 0.8146067335364524, 0.760271861001706]
```

Thank you.

issue_title: SAML2 NameID only picks first letter
issue_body:
When using the SAML2 backend, the get_attr function will return just the first letter of the nameid.
This is caused by NameID always being a single-valued field, whereas other attributes are always provided as a list, because they can have multiple values.
This means that the `attributes[key][0]` in get_attr returns the first letter.

issue_title: Consider adding --preview for destroy, refresh, and import
issue_body:
As the CLI has evolved, the `--preview` flag for the `destroy` command has been replaced by our insistence to run previews automatically. And you can skip the preview by passing `--skip-preview` OR `--yes`.

However, there is no option to _just_ see the results of the preview. e.g. what resources `pulumi refresh` bring up to date? If I imported this particular checkpoint file, what resources would be clobbered? etc.

We already support the notion of "previewing" imports, destroys, and refreshes on pulumi.com. So it's just a matter of doing the wiring on the CLI.

issue_title: [V3 Suggestion] Just suggestions
issue_body:
### Suggestions for v3 
>Staff Suggestion 

I think you should add like moderator and co owner 
Moderator wouldn't able to manage cogs and etc, but if economy is global, he could give himself and others credits, also other small non harmful commands...
Co-owner is basically like --co-owner flag in v2, but you could have multiple of them like mods... 
Also you should be able to add them with commands :/...  

>[p]dm2 

[p]dm2 would be like dm command, but it would be little bit diffrent: 
Instead of this msg: 

> ## Owner of BotName#BotDiscriminator 
> ## Hi
> You can reply to this message with ==contact 

you would get: 
Hi 

I like current dm, but i will probably need regular dm msg... 

>Editable Help 

I think in **RED V3**, you should be able to edit help... 
What i mean by edit help, is that you should be able to send cogs to categories 
For example: 
i want `[p]twitch` to be in general category and it is now in general category.

You could also hide stuff for example: 
I want to hide command `[p]8` and now regular people can't see it, only me, co-owners and mods... 


I bet you won't read it or won't add suggestion label probably...

issue_title: IDN-encode names before passing them to cryptography
issue_body: @alex sez that the next release of cryptography is going to deprecate passing U-labels into the x509 construction functions, and that passing A-labels has always been supported. So we should be sure to pass A-labels.

issue_title: "Assertion `state` failed" if using variable slice/index assignment when exporting model
issue_body:
``` python 
import torch
from torch.autograd import Variable

class DummyModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        V = Variable(torch.Tensor(2, 2))
        V[0, 0] = x
        return torch.sum(V * 3)

model = DummyModule()
x = Variable(torch.Tensor([1]))  # forward pass OK

print(model(x))
torch.onnx.export(model, (x,), 'dummy.pb')  # error
```
environment is pytorch 0.4, and the output of the above code is:
```
tensor(3.)
Traceback (most recent call last):

    return LegacyTracedModule(f, nderivs=nderivs)(*args, **kwargs)

    result = self.forward(*input, **kwargs)
```
